  • F7w5MrmYmdufprUF+EMCvA==May 26, 2026spring 2026

    I took this class in Spring 2026, and I’m pretty sure the exams are different now compared to what people who took it in 2025 experienced. The exams are technically open book, but you can only use Canvas materials — nothing else. I heard that last year people were allowed to use AI during exams, but they removed that policy this year.

    Even though it’s open book, I honestly didn’t find that very helpful because the exams are pretty tricky and cover a huge amount of material. What worked better for me was actually studying beforehand and organizing my own notes to review during the exam. You are allowed to use your own notes during the test.

    I started OMSCS in Fall 2025, and out of all the classes I’ve taken so far, this one had the best overall course structure. My background is non-CS, but I majored in math and already had some familiarity with Python, so I was able to keep up. That said, it definitely wasn’t easy. You’ll probably see some people saying this class is easy, but I’d guess most of them have a CS background. If you don’t, it’ll probably feel pretty challenging. Also, you absolutely cannot use AI for any of the coding assignments, so if you’re not from a CS background it can be rough at times. Still, if you’re already somewhat comfortable with Python and NumPy, you should be okay overall.

    There’s also a decent amount of writing involved. Honestly, if you lose points on the coding parts, the writing assignments can help balance things out a bit. I ended up getting an A, and I spent a lot of time on the writing portions. The rubrics are also very specific, which actually helped me understand what I missed in my coding and where I needed to pay more attention.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 16 hours / week

  • wzuTgVDUXQlOr0c6l20xdw==May 13, 2026spring 2026

    Course is fine... but workload is honestly insane. Class expects no AI usage despite being an AI course.

    Workload was borderline inhumane. 1 week to do both code and written reports which were on average 4-5 pages. And then on top of that, you have to worry about the final project in conjunction. So like everything just keep piling and piling up. It was very difficult for me to manage work with this.

    Rating: 3 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 35 hours / week

  • w1SovK8k43rCPMHj+/qlzg==May 12, 2026spring 2026

    I wasn't that interested in the material, so I decided to just get a B. That said, this really wasn't that hard. About half the grade is written reports, which took me about 15-25 minutes on average. There is a detailed rubric and if you follow it and make sure to answer all the questions you'll get full points most of the time. That leave a lot of room for error on the other assignments. I got about 60% on the tests and final project performance part and ended up with 88% in the class. I stopped watching the lectures after the first test and actually did better on the second test lol.

    There is a lot of extra stuff you can do for this course, so I can see why some people spend so much time on it, but if you don't want to do that, this class can be really easy if you're already pretty good at python.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 2 / 5Workload: 4 hours / week

  • EuLxTumrGEq3fqOSg9gDhQ==May 7, 2026spring 2026

    While I enjoyed the concepts from this course and plan to use them in future courses, the assignments themselves were a pain to go through. It was hard to gauge on how to improve with both the milestone and mini project assignments given the concepts in the course were so abstract, but applying them into code was completely different. Only one mini project (Monster Identification) was easy for me to solve given that the lecture for that provided some psuedocode, which is what I think this course needs. If it was up to me, I would completely redesign this course to be something closer to a technical writing/design course. While still keeping the same concepts (and possibly updating them with additional pseudocode), it would be better to give students an opportunity to learn how to design their AI agents through writing, similar to the homework assignments. Then with pseudocode included, they can take their design docs and apply them into code to ensure that their agent design is efficient enough to solve specific problems. I think that was my biggest problem of this course, the lack of direction between concept and code. Taking those concepts and writing some document of how exactly an AI agent is designed (either through specific algorithms or updated algorithms based on KBAI concepts) would have much better for students to understand how to create an AI agent, especially since we're in a time where having some sort of idea of how to design AI agents in modern AI standards is a great skillset. Again, I still plan to use and apply the concepts I've learned in this course since I think that they are interesting and useful enough for my AI journey, even if they are considered to be outdated. I just wish that this course was different in its approach in teaching how an AI agent should be designed without the lack of direction between the course's concepts and the coding assignments.

    Rating: 1 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 10 hours / week

  • JxrJucJTFXAKRome4ocviw==May 6, 2026spring 2026

    The most well-put-together course I've taken at OMSCS. I enjoyed the lecture videos, homework, and mini-projects and project milestone assignments. I found the final project to be very challenging to fully complete. The grading rubrics are clear and advertised, and the teaching staff is engaged on the class forum. The content was very interesting and scratched an itch that I had to learn "classical" AI for a long time. I wish more time was spent on the planning and logic sections, as a personal preference, and that some kind of pseudocode was provided to understand how the concepts might be implemented in code. Overall, would highly recommend to others.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 9 hours / week

  • eY/Yl9QV93aFZlDe3ehIJA==May 4, 2026spring 2026

    The final grade has not been released yet, but I expect to get an A with a high score.

    My overall takeaway is that as long as you watch the lecture videos first and understand the related concepts before doing the assignments, it is not hard to get a high grade.

    The course content itself is not difficult, but it consists mostly of scattered concepts. It feels more like a humanities course, and it is completely different from the engineering- or programming-oriented AI/ML courses that many of us may expect. Because of that, the workload feels excessive compared with the limited practical value of the course content.

    Most of the assignments felt meaningless. It was like learning 1/10 worth of useful material while putting in 12/10 worth of effort.

    Joyner added many components that may look positive on the surface but are actually not very meaningful and can feel overwhelming for students, including the reading club, peer reviews, weekly assignments, and Ed participation points. These may make the instructor seem very responsible and dedicated, but considering the actual usefulness of this course, reducing the workload would have been a better teaching decision. The ARC-AGI project was mostly meaningless busywork and had little connection to the course content. Students also posted excessively on Ed just to earn participation points.

    Rating: 1 / 5Difficulty: 1 / 5Workload: 10 hours / week

  • Ig3dqI8Bnz7o8AHlvOSzXg==April 28, 2026spring 2026

    I never learned about AI, this is so much fun and hard for beginner level who want to know about AI. I enjoy working assignments, projects and lectures. everyweek, you have something to submit, currently, before i took Exam 2, still i can manage to keep on the margin of grade A and B. I love it and recommend it whoever want to learn from the beginner level and enjoy the coding with python. you can try it.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • QXVJyKsMsSwQ8RZ4khjCCw==January 16, 2026fall 2025

    I don't really get all the complaints about this course. Some on reddit said "this is the worse class ever" while others on discord were saying that it was BS...uh...were we taking the same class? I guess one valid complaint is that the lessons aren't very applicable to industry, but like, that describes 70% of the content in this master's degree (except for GIOS, that class was amazing).

    I thought the class was pretty decent, even good at times, actually. Like sure, it's a David Joyner class so it will have a fair bit around of busy work but overall expectations were very fair and the homeworks and projects were pretty relevant and reasonable. I thought the course was altogether quite easy especially compared to summer ML4T and the TAs were very helpful.

    I wasn't a big fan of the lectures but some of the projects were fun and relevant. I personally reused my decision tree code from ML4T for one of the miniprojects and you got introductions into important algorithms like A*, DFS, BFS if you chose to use them for the miniprojects. The new ARC-AGI project was also pretty fun and while some say it was harder than the old raven's projects, I thought it was very reasonable so long as you don't procrastinate. One key thing to note is that you get points for the training problems too, so literally you are guaranteed at least 50% of the code portion of the points if you aren't lazy.

    Some complained about the TA grading but I didn't have any issues and got literally a 100% for every written assignment I submitted. All I did was open the rubric (there should be a table rubric for each assignment) and I wrote to the rubric. Put my subtitles in my paper as the exact same row name in the rubric and wrote to the rubric, and I never had a problem with grading. Heck, I thought the grading was more lenient than most of my undergraduate classes. Seriously y'all. Were we even taking the same class?

    Overall, 10 to 15 hours a week for this class as long as you are consistent. Pretty moderately easy. I think hardest parts were a few of the mini-projects and milestone D of the ARC-AGI projects, but if you do well enough on the mini-projects you can afford to half-heartedly address milestone D.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 2 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • +P2SNPgxTxx8N5phkJLrpA==January 16, 2026fall 2025

    This course was my first OMSCS class. I came away with mostly positive feelings about the class.

    For starters - Dr. Joyner and the TA we excellent. They were super responsive, engaged, and enthusiastic. I think that the decision to migrate the semester project to ARC-AGI from Raven's Progressive Matrices was awesome. I appreciate how much work that must have taken - and I feel that it greatly enhanced the learning experience.

    Another positive aspect of the course is how well organized it is. It is clear from Day 1 exactly what you need to be successful in the class. Everything is available from the jump and this would be a great class to work ahead in if you wanted to. There are many assignments throughout the semester. While this can be a bit grating, or feel like busywork at times, they were generally interesting. And opportunities for easy points. Sometimes other reviews for other courses discuss being annoyed with the uncertainty - and I feel like this class is the antithesis of that.

    The lectures I found interesting at times. Unfortunately, they didn't feel as "rigorous" as I wanted them to. I feel like there are more abstract topics in computer science (like algorithms) are theoretically/mathematically well founded. Or there are more practical classes (like Operating Systems) which are grounded in their practical application in the real world. KBAI feels like a course discussing a particular view of artificial intelligence which is neither mathematically "true" nor broadly in use. It is a very "vibey" class which kinda left me feeling like I was not learning a "real" computer science topic.

    Some people in the class complained about the peer review software or some of the other administration of the class - but that never felt like an issue to me. Also - people who were complaining about not getting perfect scores on the assignments seemed to be missing the forest (an A is extremely attainable in this course) through the trees (missing 5% due to poor formatting on a report without comprehensive enough TA explanations).

    In summary - I think this is one of the best ran classes I have ever taken. It really does not waste your time teaching you the material along the way. Ultimately - the subject matter / topic did not completely resonant with me. Which colors my rating + feelings of the class. But depending on what you want out of it - it could be a perfect class.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 8 hours / week

  • 2c1btKEcjhtxtHHB8f2rMg==December 24, 2025fall 2025

    Background: 1st semester student, Undergrad CS major, working as a Data Scientist for less than a year.

    Finished the course a month early and ended up with an A(91.89%).

    Pros:

    • The lectures are very interesting and enjoyable, and they are presented in a way that’s easy to understand.
    • The coding assignments aren’t too difficult and are manageable.
    • The TA and Dr. Joyner are great and respond quickly when help is needed.
    • The integration with gradescope is cool, you are able to submit coding assignments up to 40 times and it auto grades so you can see your grade right away. Cons:
    • The lectures don’t align very closely with the assignments; the assignments aren’t really AI focused and feel more like general coding tasks.
    • The writing assignments feel somewhat useless and repetitive more like busywork that could be avoided.
    • I received feedback on early assignments, but later in the course I didn’t get any feedback at all.

    Homework(15% )- 90/100: These are writing assignments (journals) based primarily on lecture material. Make sure to follow the prompt questions closely and answer them clearly and completely. Demonstrate a strong understanding of the relevant lecture concepts and apply them accurately. When a prompt asks for a diagram, ensure that it matches the one shown in the lecture exactly. In a previous assignment, I mixed up two concepts and received no credit for either, which resulted in a C on that assignment so accuracy is very important.

    Exams: Midterm(7.5%) - 70.91/100, Final(7.5%) - 92.72/100: I’m not a great test taker, so I didn’t do very well on the exams. They aren’t too hard since they are open notes and open internet including AI, but you still need to understand the concepts really well. On the first exam I struggled with time management and ended up turning to ChatGPT as a last resort, which didn’t help much. After that poor result, I made really good notes and used NotebookLM by Google to ask questions based on them for the second exam, and I ended up doing much better. I recommend that if you are unsure if a statement is correct to leave it unselected.

    Mini Projects: Performance(15%) - 98.5/100, Journals(15%) - 95.4/100: The coding assignments are fairly easy and similar to medium-level LeetCode problems, so they shouldn’t take too long, except for Mini-Project 2 (Block World), which was difficult and cost me a few points. Overall, the assignments are manageable. The writing assignments are short (max 4 pages), but I lost points for not including metrics related to efficiency and performance.

    ARC-AGI project: Performance(7.5%) - 100/100, Journals(7.5%) - 100/100: For the assignments, you’ll be solving ARC-AGI problems by creating an agent. I didn’t implement any real AI methods myself, what I did was design a specific solution approach for each problem. To achieve full credit on each milestone, you need to pass at least 6 out of 16 general test cases and 6 out of 16 hidden test cases. The journals can be repetitive, so feel free to reuse the same structure each time. Just make sure you include specific metrics for each milestone, including: Efficiency: Big-O complexity and actual runtime, Performance: Number of test cases passed Keep in mind that even though you only need 6/16 for both general and hidden tests at each milestone, you’ll eventually need to solve all of them for the final project. Because of that, I recommend completing as many problems as possible throughout the milestones rather than waiting until the end.

    Final ARC-AGI project: Performance(7.5%) - 84.38/100, Journals(7.5%) - 76/100: Each milestone (Milestones B through D) includes 16 general tests and 16 hidden tests, for a total of 48 general tests and 48 hidden tests across all milestones. I wasn’t able to solve 15 of the hidden tests, which left me with a performance score of 81/96. My performance wasn’t as strong as it could’ve been because I decided it wasn’t worthwhile to spend hours trying to figure out each hidden test for just a 1 point increase. The final journal grading is much stricter than the other journals. I answered every question on the assignment, but some parts were considered vague by the TAs, which lowered my score. I put in the same level of effort as I did for every milestone, where I earned 100 out of 100, but the TAs expected more for the final journal. I recommend being very clear and keeping in mind that the grading is stricter.

    Participation(10%) - 100 / 100: These points are basically free, and there are plenty of opportunities to earn them. I mainly completed peer reviews each week to stay ahead. After about two months, I had already earned the full 90/90 points and didn’t have to think about it for the rest of the semester.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 12 hours / week

  • 11Wwcn5oDMuArd9QfJEb5w==November 21, 2025spring 2025

    I found the lectures interesting but not terribly helpful. I really like the professor and admire the effort he puts in to provide a good learning experience and a great class though. The goal of the lectures is to teach first principles to use solving ARC AGI problems, but because there's not a lot of machine learning from data it feels less like an AI course and more like a few dozen leetcode problems. Some concepts were more advanced, like incremental concept learning (decision trees updated one data point at a time) but many AI concepts felt more like I was the only intelligence involved (represent problem with a state space, use guess and check to solve, etc). Most of the hours I spent on the class were on the semester long project, with took me around 100 hours. In retrospect I think it would have been faster and easier to ignore the course concepts and write a separate algorithm for each of the 48 types of puzzles than trying to build a general solver that uses course concepts like I did. The tests weren't fun but they felt pretty pair for other GT classes, and the rest of the mini projects were pretty quick. The course has a significant participation grade that required reviewing other peoples reports (required with each coding project). I wasn't a huge fan of that aspect, and felt like the reviews were not helpful, but they weren't too inconvenient.

    Rating: 3 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 12 hours / week

  • +vrBbYGMp/QLf7FttbIhIw==August 23, 2025summer 2025

    I enjoyed this course. It helped me frame my perspective about AI Agents. The foundations on how an AI Agent need to be built is interesting. Newly introduced ARC AGI is fun. Summer was little short in duration to do ARC AGI project in truly KBAI manner. However, I feel I have broadened my thinking and knowledge in this field after this course.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 12 hours / week

  • 2X4gT7/0utSv0tP6WcTDUw==August 4, 2025summer 2025

    TL;DR: - OVerall, I liked the course. There is a lot of work involved. TAs are good, there needs to be better connection between lecture material and projects, you will write a lot and the participation portion is not that bad.

    I took this over the summer and paired it with AIES thinking a medium and an easy class would go well together. Overall, I was correct. Lots of people have outlined the course contents and what you need to do. For a point of reference, I finished with a 93% for the course. Here are some thoughts on the course:

    1. You will write a lot of reports. I found them to be better than I expected. You are given a rubric to follow. If you follow the rubric, format the report so that sections match the rubric sections and you answer the questions in the rubric in each section where they are asked (even if you occasionally repeat yourself) you will be fine. After the first one or two, you get the hang of what they want. I can't prove it but adding in diagrams and flowcharts seem to help the grade.

    2. There is a lot of (busy)work. Canvas shows 31 graded assignments ranging from a few course surveys to the final exam and final project. You will be busy with multiple things due each week.

    3. There is a lot of "noise" in the course. Ed is filled with lots of extra posts on topical things that are not required but meant to build an online community and discuss ideas. You can decide if they are worth participating in.

    4. The lectures are somewhat dated and Dr.Joyner is working on updates. He released a few of them this semester for us to preview and comment on.

    5. The lectures are very much at the 30,000 ft. level. They cover a topic with examples using Ed Lessons. When you are done, you know how they work but there is no practical "how would I implement this?" or "What does the algorithm for that look like?"

    6. The final project - ARC-AGI was a lot of fun. Unfortunately, many students (including myself) use a more brute force approach to solving the project because there isn't any guidance on how we might connect the dots from the 30,000 ft. level to the 10 ft. level of implementation. On the project implementation I got a 73%. Because the project implementation weight is only 7.5%, it wasn't worth the remaining hours I had left to get to 100%. Time was better spent on the report (which I got a 100% on) which isn't directly linked to how well you did the implementation but is heavily linked to how well you can explain why you chose the method you did, where it worked well, where it struggled, etc. I suggested they cut the project form 96 test cases to 60 for the summer due to the compressed schedule.

    7. Participation - You need 90 points to get full participation credit. If you participate on the forums, ask questions, answer questions, make comments, you can get 40 points. If you join one of the reading groups and make it to all five meetings, you can get 25 points. They have a wide range of books and I found this to be a highlight of the course. That leaves you with 25 points to get. Each peer review that you leave if done in a timely manner is 1.5 points and they take about ten minutes to write. My suggestion is to do reviews on the ARC-AGI reports that you write for each of the five deliverables. You can do extra ones up front and get done early. This is a good way to see how others are implementing their project and get ideas you can use for yours. You can get the last 25 points from doing 17 or 18 peer reviews. I found them to be helpful.

    8. With so many small assignments, one bad assignment is not going to kill your grade. I see this as a positive.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 20 hours / week

  • FNE7KG9UHITnWcBoMYKKlQ==May 7, 2025spring 2025

    This course is an absolute nice choice for those choosing the AI track (formerly the II track). After taking it, I found it to be significantly easier than the ML and AI courses, and getting an A is quite achievable. I spent about 4–8 hours per week on this course. Only one or two of the mini-projects were relatively challenging. I recommend that those who don't want to spend too much time directly take a case-by-case approach for the AGI project, rather than trying to develop a strong generalized AI method (this is for efficiency — if you’re passionate about KBAI, then you should go for it).

    As for the exam, since it allows the use of any online resources, I barely prepared and completed it using AI (which is permitted). The exam score generally doesn’t have a decisive impact; as long as you perform well on other assignments, a score of 70 on the exam is acceptable (in my opinion, this is the lower bound for using AI without your own thinking). Similarly, it’s hard to rely on the exam alone to pull your grade back up to an A.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 2 / 5Workload: 6 hours / week

  • KCXPaUVMNdTn9FFaBtCk/g==May 5, 2025spring 2025

    Finished with 93%. Projects are doable, and difficulty is manageable. Aim to collect max points for each assignment, and you will end up with A. The actual ARC-AGI problem becomes difficult as you progress through the semester, so it will be difficult to collect the full points later. If you follow the Slack and Ed Forum, other students usually give a lot of hints on how to solve problems.

  • Mw7Pcg1v1ZizqZ+0Ny9c0g==May 2, 2025spring 2025

    This is my first OMSCS course (planning on a double concentration in ML and II). My background is a software Engineer for almost 2 years, did Undergrad Major: Management information Systems, minor: computer science. Only class I took this semester.

    For an intro-course to grad school and getting my brain in learning shape, this was a great course for that. Wasn't exactly easy, but not too rigorous that I would be destroyed. Initially was spending about 5 ours more than the average I selected, as I took my time to be extra careful and wanted an A to give me that confidence boost, starting Grad school. Spent less time around the middle of the semester as i had gotten acclimated to it and some assignments were objectively easier. In terms of usefulness, not super confident what the real world applications of some of the lessons learned, but does introduce you to thinking patterns, libraries, etc that i think will be super helpful in more advanced courses down the line.

    Rating: 3 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 14 hours / week

  • Y27reY917wo7TVDiLgNNWA==April 25, 2025spring 2025

    I thought this course was alright. My impression was content isn't very hard to understand, so they overload you with busy work to make us feel like the course is difficult. Some of the assignments are helpful, and some are just extra. Grading at times seemed arbitrary with insufficient and/or repetitive feedback given by TAs, despite the fact that rubrics are public. That being said, I don't know if this turned out to be that difficult of a course (though Exam 2 and final paper grades are outstanding, so maybe it's too early to say) all things considered. In addition, for what it's worth, Joyner did seem gung ho about getting feedback, but it's definitely too early to say that's a good sign, as instructors gathering feedback and instructors acting on it are two very different things.

    Rating: 3 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 10 hours / week

  • yGQfqHWI/Zm/+Be0x+tsuQ==April 23, 2025spring 2025

    I greatly enjoyed this course. Ill break this review in to lectures, instructors, and assignments.

    Lectures: 4/5 The lectures are engaging, interesting, and well taught in my opinion. Its a Dr. Joyner class, so if you like the EdLesson style he uses, you'll be right at home. I really liked Dr. Ashoks lecturing, and the cognitive science tie ins are very interesting. My only complaint is that the lectures are very high level, focusing only on concepts, and leaving implementation of all assignments and projects to the student with little guidance. If youre well versed in python and numpy this is okay, but if not, consider brushing up first.

    Instructors: 5/5 The instructors wand TAs were awesome. They were very helpful, responsive, and reasonable. They take the time to answer all major questions on the forums, and have some grace/understanding when severe circumstances cause the need for an extension or similar.

    Assignments: 4/5 There are homeworks, mini projects, and a course project. The homeworks felt a bit useless, at least half of them did. I breezed through pretty easily on that front. The mini projects were a bit more substantial and felt a lot more interesting and related to lecture. They had a wild range in difficulty with the shortest one taking me ~20 minutes to complete with an A, and the longest taking north of 10 hours, so watch out for that. The course project has changed from Ravens progressive matrices to the ARCAGI challenge this semester. With it being the first semester, it was a little rocky, but I still learned a lot and had a great time. There are a lot of kinks to work out, but the instructors are asking for feedback, and seem very open to addressing these issues moving forward, so I am very confident this will mature in to an awesome course project after another couple semesters of trial and error.

    Overall, with a background in CS and ML, I had no issue getting an A with 8-10 hours per week. but I did see people on the forums talking about taking 15+ hours to get a B. Because of this, I would say taking a more intro level AI or ML course first would be smart if you dont have a formal CS background like an undergrad degree. I would say that the new project is a lot more time consuming than the old project, so be weary of older reviews that suggest this as a first OMSCS course or an easy A. The instructors make the grading requirements very clear, and they are quite fair, so an A or B is absolutely attainable as long as you are on top of the work.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 9 hours / week

  • P9G23VLKmD7PR7uy2fntGQ==April 20, 2025spring 2025

    I'm currently finishing the class and I'd say that it was an excellent first introduction to OMSCS! The class is somewhat intensive but not to a mind-numbing degree and the projects can be somewhat fun.

    There's three main parts: the mini-projects, the homework, and the final projects. There's also two exams.

    The mini-projects are fairly fun. The first three are fairly difficult with the first two relying on array/logic manipulation, the third being solely NLP, and the fourth and fifth being a breeze with some people reportedly finishing the fourth with only about ten lines of code. Most of the projects also score you partially on accuracy as well as efficiency, so if you reach the correct answer but not in the optimal number of steps then you may lose points. You'll also need to write a paper for each project which will be half of the points for that project.

    The homeworks are hit or miss. If you're the kind of person to miss instructions often, then make sure to read them very thoroughly. Otherwise, the lectures are helpful to review for these.

    The final project is the new ARC-AGI challenge and it seems like the general consensus is that it's just a bunch of leetcode challenges tied together with a detection system. Most people are currently aiming for about 75% for this one, so the difficulty is definitely fairly high. You'll be writing papers for these too. I didn't hate the project, but make sure to complete as much of the Milestones as you can before the final project is due.

    Overall, great first class! I think it would give anyone coming in a nice introduction to OMSCS as their first course, as it's mildly rigorous without completely pulling the rug out from beneath you. You'll need a strong understanding of Python and Numpy though.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • zgMHxawDF1NZxVYd8Iu4aw==December 31, 2024spring 2024

    Full review here! https://the11d.wordpress.com/2024/12/31/my-thoughts-on-kbai-omscs-review-6/

    TLDR (Thanks GPT!)

    CS 7637 (Knowledge-Based Artificial Intelligence) in OMSCS provides a deep dive into classical AI from a cognitive science perspective, emphasizing symbolic reasoning over statistical methods. The course features a rigorous workload, including weekly assignments, open-ended mini-projects, proctored exams, and a semester-long project to build an AI agent for solving Raven’s Progressive Matrices (RPM). Students need strong Python and writing skills, with challenges like Mini-Project 2 requiring significant logical problem-solving and creativity. While some content feels outdated and involves busywork, the course’s engaging lectures, unique problem-solving opportunities, and focus on foundational AI concepts make it highly rewarding for those interested in classical AI approaches. The reviewer rated it 3.8/5 for quality and 3.1/5 for difficulty, recommending it for students eager to explore cognitive science and symbolic AI.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 9 hours / week

  • z/vxkQ2fB0dpqkCtO8H7pw==December 31, 2024fall 2024

    This is one of the worst course I would say. They have lots of documentation and peer reviews you need to do. Almost 55 marks in useless peer reviews and documentation. Rest 45 marks is the one you would gain from the actual course. This is just lame course where you would not get any help and have to rely on yourself alone. I would rate this as 0

    Rating: 1 / 5Difficulty: 1 / 5Workload: 20 hours / week

  • sTsralZklUgAsWN86aZZzA==December 16, 2024fall 2024

    Avoid this class if you can!

    This isn't a tough class but it has a ridiculous amount of work. Weekly papers to write and coding. Some coding assignments are very easy, you can finish them in a few hours. Mini-Project 2 took me weeks of coding and I couldn't get a high score.

    You can front load this course since pretty much everything is released in the first few weeks. I got a lot of stuff done initially and then got stuck with Mini-Project 2 and had to keep up with the weekly load.

    I didn't do too well on the Final Project unfortunately and I had to focus on getting a good grade on the Final Exam. I only wanted a B in this class but I could have easily gotten an A if I wanted to.

    Again, avoid this class if you can. You will be fine if you do take it, just start early and stay on top of things.

    Rating: 2 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 12 hours / week

  • lVaErvC+H+S2COrzPeOalw==December 15, 2024fall 2024

    Finished the course with an A, achieving a 94%

    Background:

    • Bachelor's degree in Computer Science from a university ranked #377 out of 436 National Universities in U.S. News
    • English is my second language (TOEFL score: 95/120)
    • 1 year of experience as a full-stack developer

    Overall:

    • The lecture is easy to understand, thanks to the excellent course design (one of the best-designed courses compared to others)
    • The concepts in this class are very abstract, focusing more on high-level design rather than low-level coding. If you're interested in designing AI systems, this is a great class to take. However, if your goal is to focus on coding, consider switching specializations and avoiding this course
    • This class includes a writing section that requires you to produce "graduate-level" papers. From the peer feedback, it’s clear that some people do not meet the required standards (Follow the rubric and explain your work in detail)

    Homework (13.75 / 15%): Create a diagram to help explain the concept clearly. Avoid relying solely on plain text; the TA might deduct points if the explanation isn't engaging enough. Image the grader is likely at a high school level, so your explanation should be simple and easy to follow

    Exams (6.07 / 7.5% - midterm, 5.45 / 7.5% - final): I’m not a strong test or exam taker. While the tests aren’t necessarily hard, they can be tricky and require a solid understanding of the concepts. Personally, I found some of the English questions confusing, which made things even more challenging. Fortunately, the exams don’t carry much weight in the overall grade, which was a relief. The class average is typically around 88–92 out of 110.

    Mini Project(14.93 / 15% - Performance, 14.93 / 15% Journals): Start working as soon as the materials are released. The assignments are very straightforward if you're familiar with LeetCode, as they are comparable to easy-to-medium questions. The only point I missed was Project 2: Block World Agent—it’s difficult to optimize this project effectively. Additionally, I forgot to include the Gradescope score in my journal submission. Make sure to start Project 3: Sentence Reading Agent early to stay on track

    One big projects(14.06 / 15% - Performance, 14.81 / 15% - Journals): Read the guidelines carefully—many students skip the syllabus and end up working on challenge questions, only to realize at the end of the semester that those don’t count toward the grade. Focus on solving the questions that are part of the grading system first. If you want to take on the extra challenges, feel free, but make sure your priorities are clear. There are often complaints from students about going in the wrong direction, but the expectations are clearly explained at the start of the semester. Overall, the project is challenging but manageable. The instructor has stated that it’s difficult to achieve 100% on this project, so don’t stress too much about perfection. Aim for a solid score and dedicate your time to other tasks as well.

    Participation(10 / 10%): These are essentially free points, and there are plenty of ways to earn them. However, many people still complain at the end of the semester that it’s hard to achieve. The key is to start early in the semester and carefully read the syllabus to understand how to earn these points. It only took me 30 minutes over the first 12 weeks to get 100% in this section, finish it early, and save time for the final project

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • Nv/cUqz/VWjhuBzXzesP/g==December 12, 2024fall 2024

    I deeply enjoyed this class. My background is as an architect who was promoted out of hands-on development into management a while ago - and I miss coding. This was my first OMSCS class and I found this class excellent for shaking off some programming and academic rust. I also genuinely enjoyed the topic.

    In my opinion this class is a good choice for two groups of people:

    • People interested in specifically exploring AI that thinks like humans (i.e. the subject of the class)
    • People looking for an enjoyable and well-run class to get them into the swing of OMSCS.

    Despite this being a very popular class in the program this class’s main topic is actually a bit niche - AI techniques to specifically mimic how humans think isn’t the current most popular branch of AI (that’s Machine Learning) but I do feel these ‘classical’ techniques may provide some potentially interesting avenues for AI in the future.

    The Good:

    • Class is very well-run with a lot of engagement from the professor, responsive TAs, and clear expectations.
    • Mini-Projects are fun and a good balance of being interesting without being too challenging.
    • You can absolutely work ahead in this class.
    • The class feels extremely fair - if you’re willing to put in the work you can absolutely achieve an ‘A’
    • There’s supplemental material available if you’re at all interested in the topic - lots of additional stuff to explore.

    The Bad:

    • The class is just a lot of work - it’s ‘only’ 12-15 hours a week but you’ll be actively producing deliverables (coding, papers, peer reviews) for almost all of that, and some of it (esp in the homeworks) can feel very busy-work. Getting the homework into the page length requirement can be harder and take more time than the homework topic.
    • RPM is easier to implement using CV heuristics rather than class topics - I’ve heard they may change this project in the future which I do agree with, because while it’s a lot of fun as a project it’s much harder to use KBAI techniques rather than just bypass them and use CV heuristics.

    The Controversial: Some students seem to feel the later lectures are ‘just simplified cognitive science’. I’m not sure where this idea comes from - these lectures cover architectures for KBAI (Explanation Based Learning, Version Space Learning, Commonsense Reasoning, etc.). I think because the techniques aren’t implemented in code during the class some students don’t really connect the abstract techniques/architectures to how one would practically implement one of these systems. So depending on your level of interest in the specific subject (KBAI) these lectures are either going to be the most interesting or most boring part of the class.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • +nzeCBUmALuR4svysa4e/w==December 11, 2024fall 2024

    Me: Undergrad in CS. Fresh graduate. I'm a full-time student. I took this alongside HCI. It's my first semester at GaTech.

    Grade: A (95%)

    It's a good course. I'm not sure how relevant the concepts are any more, but an interesting course nonetheless. Ed Discussions are fun and informative. TAs are helpful. Rubrics are clear, so it's easy to score full mark on assignments. If you have some experience in python and coding, especially an undergrad in CS, this course should be very easy. Report writing can get annoying, but it's not hard. Start assignments early and it's easy to stay ahead. Staying ahead will give you a lot of buffer room. Especially if you finish early, since everything is available from the beginning.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 2 / 5Workload: 8 hours / week

  • 1OH+fPR2qV+jaLfsUVKZdQ==December 7, 2024spring 2024

    KBAI is a good intro class for the II track in that it warms up the two kinds of things which you'll need to get ready to do in a graduate school program which is a mix of ML/AI and HCI: understand requirements and clearly write about your understanding and implementations.

    I have heard (substantiated) rumors that they're changing the core project (Ravens Cognitive Matrices) to something else. For me, that project was something that I "went ahead" with in the beginning and obsessed with it for a bit, did ok enough, and then never touched it again. It's interesting, but I found it interesting in a short dedicated effort kind of way rather than a longer set of small effort kind of way. Maybe that's just me. The projects varied, but I have no complaints. It felt like they added closed book/note quizzes because that's what classes do; I don't think they added much, and I think the main thing they did was to stress me out before taking them. But overall, a whirlwind tour of some (generally) non probabilistic AI methods; could be inspiring for someone interested in intepretability?

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 10 hours / week

  • 0qNdCyeDuSTnuVthrbLt9g==October 16, 2024spring 2024

    NOTE: DO NOT TAKE THIS CLASS!

    I haven’t reviewed any of my other classes, but I’m making an exception for this one because I disliked it so much that I feel compelled to warn others. I expected this course to be an enjoyable, easy experience, like CS-6795 (Intro to Cognitive Science), but instead, it focuses on basic concepts and how AI agents mimic human behavior. Unfortunately, you'll spend your time on pointless tasks: 5 mini-projects, 3 homework assignments, 4 milestones for a major project, and 2 exams. Every week, you're also required to peer review three classmates' papers, but most people barely skim them for participation points. The projects are tedious and offer no real learning value—you won’t be working on anything cutting-edge or relevant to your future career. For each assignment, you’ll end up writing around 5 pages, totaling 65-70 pages for the entire course. If you're serious about learning something that will advance your career, do not take this class. I wish I had read the reviews before enrolling.

    Rating: 1 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 28 hours / week

  • ZduXZVe+NcBIRDua2dy92A==August 7, 2024spring 2024

    TLDR: Do NOT take this course - you will spend an incredible amount of time but learn next to nothing out of it.

    This has been an absolutely worst class for me, not only in the program but probably among the other 20+ graduate courses I've taken throughout my life. There was maybe a course or two where I learned less but then they required much less work. There were courses with higher workload, but then I learned much more from them. This course feels like an incredible amount of busy but absolutely useless work. Even the lectures: very lengthy but with minimal breadth. A student in our class summarized the content of the entire course in a 19 page PDF and that was all in it. And even this summarized material has very little to no practical use. Some mini-projects and the course-long project were kind of fun to work on but they had nothing to do with the course material. Also, keep in mind that each programming assignment should be accompanied with a write-up. On top of that, there are 3 homeworks that are purely write-ups. So it feels that you are taking an English class, not a CS one. To complete the picture, the questions on the exams were also worded in an incomprehensible way. The only good thing in this course for me was that working on the course-long project I got an intro to OpenCV that I wanted to learn. Finally, if you care about your grade (that I was not much though somehow managed to get an A), the written parts are graded absolutely arbitrarily and no re-grade request were acknowledged.

    Rating: 1 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 40 hours / week

  • PoQ3UPp0U2AnwzOB/7IwRw==July 30, 2024summer 2024

    My Background: B.S. in CS from a reputable university. 4 YOE as a SWE. I've taken CS 7646 and 6310. I found CS 7646 was good preparation for this course, but consider CS 7646 more difficult than this one in many ways.

    Course Performance: Safe 'A'.

    Recommended Background: Have Python experience before taking this course as its first projects require OOP Python from the get go with no build up. Know your graph algorithms - especially breadth-first search. There are courses on edX by Georgia Tech that can provide the required Python background for this course if you don't have prior experience.

    Tips to Do Well in the Course: 1.) To get full participation points when reviewing classmates' papers, use the grading rubric for the assignment to structure your response. 2.) Write your project journals based off of the grading rubric for time efficiency and to ensure you cover all required items. 3.) Scoring well on the final project's performance section will take way more effort than other assignments. Accept that your grade is going to take a hit on the final project and ensure you do well on all assignments before the final project. 4.) For mini-projects you don't necessarily need to worry too much about efficiency. Try a simple, brute force approach first then seek to optimize your solution. 5.) Take page-numbered notes during the lectures and organize them by a table of contents. Reference those notes and ctrl-f search the transcripts of the video lessons for exams for any questions you're unsure on. The timing of the exams was nice and not intensely pressured like many OMSCS courses. 6.) Do Peer Feedback tasks and do them early. You get 3 times as many points for doing them early. Try to do as many as possible in the beginning of the course to frontload the workload for the course as much as you can. 7.) Prioritize doing extra peer feedback tasks for the RPM project so you can learn from your classmate's approaches. Keep a google doc of notes of things you learn from your classmates that you may want to incorporate. 8.) Participate in the EdDiscussions in ways that are enjoyable for you. For me that was answering classmates' questions and participating in the Wednesday Debates. 9.) Pay attention to JDF format! Ensure your margins and font size are correct. They mostly care grade-wise if you're using margins or font sizes that allow you to write more than other students. Create a Google Doc template that follows JDF format and make a copy of this before each assignment to efficiently ensure you follow the required report format. 10.) Monitor EdDiscussions. The course is set up to be very collaborative. Learn from your classmates and their posts.

    Course Thoughts:

    The course material is very interesting. Knowledge-based AI is more intuitive in that you can understand how an agent arrives at its answers. This course provides interesting methodologies to allow your agents (programs) to learn that differ from machine learning. The course feels like it supplements an algorithms course in that it explores problem-solving strategies and gives a more intuitive language to algorithms than I had encountered before.

    The course is well-organized and the expectations are clear. The course has a demanding workload, but I would not say it's overly difficult as some of the posts here state. Start your assignments early and do not procrastinate. All course material and assignments are available from day one.

    Course Critique:

    1.) The homework assignments are busy work. Their material is already covered by the mini-projects. I think the level of work for the course is appropriate except for these homework assignments. 2.) The course project revolving around Raven's Progressive Matrices (RPM) is really interesting! Unfortunately, the need to learn vision processing to apply the course concepts encourages much easier, gimmicky strategies that do much better. I think this course could be greatly improved if the vision processing necessary to create conceptual models and analogical mappings of the RPM problems was taught as part of the course. This would help students that want to take that approach to apply the concepts from the course more. Otherwise, the concepts from the course get rather disconnected from the final project through the use of DPR, IPR, and case recording which score extremely well but don't really apply course concepts. Meanwhile, actual vision processing based strategies applying the course concepts require way more work and don't perform as well.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 20 hours / week

  • sriKQMcJ2p6HVPM/lkANFw==July 30, 2024summer 2024

    The semester long project was very rewarding and fun to work on - but it was worth very little of your total grade. 7.5% for the report, 7.5% for the actual code. Getting 90-100 percent on coding assignments didn't take much effort (and were fun!), but the written reports accompanying the homework assignments are graded according to a rubric that is ---not--- 1:1 to the assignment instructions.

    Make sure to read the report rubrics and title sections/bold phrases exactly as they appear in the rubric. <---------

    If you do the first few assignments without realizing this, you will lose a good percent of your grade. Especially in Summer - you will be turning in multiple assignments before you get your first batch of grades. Given written reports (outside the final report) are 47.5% of your grade, getting 2-3 bad grades without realizing this can significantly impact your grade and make it unnecessarily difficult.

    You don't really need any pre-reqs for this course - know some basic Python and you're okay - this is a good intro course.

    tl;dr I recommend the course and found the content fun and rewarding, but be aware that the project and tests are not the most important grade-wise.

    Rating: 3 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • 4iQIAB0t31C17g8CQ01FNg==July 29, 2024summer 2024

    The lectures are enjoyable and thought provoking, but not very informative and very abstract. it doesnt map to algorithms most of the time. The negatives here are as follows, the course provides no exxtra value than watching the videos on youtube or without actually enrolling to the course. There are no office hours , tutorials nor TA engagment like other courses, grading is alway super late, and feedback is a few words so not helpful to learn from TA correction. Also course load is way more than the learning value as you have to write and review alot, which doesnt help the learning process as much as they portray it. This course relies on the fact that class participation is graded so that people help each other rather than TAs supportting which in my view didnt work much. Its easy to get an A if you put the effort although effort does weight well aganist the amount of learning. Last nitpick is you re betteroff implementing most projects from you prior algorithm knowledge than trying to implement philosphies in lecture and after just attempt to link them together which is also disappointing somewhat since course knowledge is not needed for any of the project even the final ravens project. for final project go for simple pixel and image difference approaches saves you so much effort and time from trying to apply course shape relationships and will get you a higher grade.

    Rating: 2 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 12 hours / week

  • 8KnyeiIGQWsSiiZILHJ79g==July 24, 2024summer 2024

    Horrible class....the concepts are good but the staff is absolutely abysmal. Students are setup for failure. It is quite evident in almost every GT OMSCS class. You can literally see students crying and complaining but the staff takes no interest and just says "Yeah well thats the standard of GT". They are right... bad teaching is a standard at GT. Students are setup for failure here....Dont do OMSCS. Go to a school that cares about your mental health. So many better schools like UT Austin, Purdue etc. where they actually care about what matters and not stupid stuff.

    Rating: 1 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 40 hours / week

  • nzCXg5JowGYIPWMssU2N0w==June 27, 2024summer 2023

    Review #2 Summer 2024 learned nothing, forced to see psychotherapist 3 times, chiropractors 5 times. Dropped

    One question: Why do I want to read the garbage other people has written using chatgpt 3x or 6x every week?

    Rating: 1 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 30 hours / week

  • w9iGYiqFBOv9Fi4z2Wt8FA==June 20, 2024spring 2024

    Took it as my first course while working full time. I do have a decent amount of experience in software but not with Python, which is the language we used for all of our projects in this class. wrapping up with python and getting familiar with some of the libraries look some time but was not the main problem. This course has ALOT of weekly tasks which makes it a very difficult first class. On top of trying to get back into school, especially an online school, and learning how to use Python to deliver the projects which one is due almost every week, we had to write 5 page papers weekly, do at least 3 peer reviews, watch 2-3 lessons that were sort of boring, midterm, final, and a very difficult final project which we gradually build during the whole class timeline that hold a pretty big chunk of the overall grade .I had a 90% and it dropped to 86% because the TA gave me a super low grade on the paper, although I had been receiving mostly A's on all my papers.

    Its a good class, but I wouldn't suggest it as the first class due to overload of assignments due per week.

    Rating: 3 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 20 hours / week

  • ntdlxje+NLk4rRE2nVpOiQ==June 18, 2024summer 2023

    Feels like another crappy course so far

    Rating: 1 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 20 hours / week

  • H8UW4Q96wlD9inQqa+Oe+w==June 1, 2024spring 2024

    This was my first course in OMSCS, I thought it was a solid introduction and ramped me up well. I had a solid professional background in software going in (undergrad in CS, SWE full-time) and found the coding assignments provided reasonable value and were very approachable.

    The most valuable part of the course was the context of AI, the introduction to and histories of the topic, and the approach of development for various solutions. The term-long project can be daunting at first, but was a fun challenge by the end of the term - it's very approachable if you build cleanly from the start and meet the milestones as recommended.

    There was a fair amount of writing, probably 50-60 total pages of reporting in JDF throughout the course. This was my first time using the format and it's quite approachable. The rubrics for these assignments were very refined and it was easy to get full marks by referring to the rubric and assignment description - they explicitly state what they're looking for.

    Lastly, the staff were extremely helpful and very organized. In contrast to some other courses, they're on another level in terms of successful course management.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • DTG5zt0goEu1YWof6DKxUQ==May 14, 2024spring 2024

    WHAT A WASTE OF TIME! This was my 7th course in the program and by far was my least favorite. I did not learn anything at all. The lectures are so boring. All my time wasted for tedious reports and peer reviews. There are a couple of interesting mini-projects at the beginning but the rest are so useless. Homeworks are the worst part of the deliverables. Tip: For peer reviews, write a lot and do not just write the three required ones. Go for extra peer reviews if you want to get a good participation point. I lost some points since allegedly my reviews were not detailed enough.

    Rating: 1 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 12 hours / week

  • EO2e8iO8VHkbKLROCc6Pxg==May 2, 2024fall 2023

    This is the BEST OMSCS course I've taken so far. This to know:

    • There is a ton of writing and peer reviews. Very similar to HCI.
    • There is a semester-long project to build an AI Agent. You can find tons of information about this all over the internet.
    • An 'A' is possible with 10-15 hours of work each week.
    • There are mini-projects throughout the course that can be brute forced. Don't overthink them. You're trying to get an A, not impress a leetcode interviewer.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • 5B+OBPZbth9LTScRvJn1cQ==April 17, 2024fall 2023

    This was my first course in OMSCS.

    Honestly a really well-structured course, it becomes very apparent that they have been considerate of different learning styles and different viewpoints. Lectures are impressively well-put together. Instructor and TAs were really accessible and responsive. Content was pretty easy to digest, and complexity of assignments were very fair (sometimes too easy).

    However...terribly time consuming. Almost weekly, we had to do some coding assignment, write a paper about it, and do some peer reviews on top of that. While it's still quite an easy course, sometimes it feels like there's very little time for a break, so work ahead whenever possible!

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 20 hours / week

  • tiCeFqq+i+v2bZk+eIR6CA==December 30, 2023fall 2023

    My background: I am a software engineer with a few years experience, and I have a BS in Computer Science.

    In general, this class was fairly interesting, but nothing too exciting or challenging. The main course project revolves around solving problems in "Ravens Progressive Matrices" (those IQ test type questions with 3 images, and you have to solve for the fourth one). This project was fun to screw around with, but it was not really related to any of the lectures. I was able to get ~70/96 by just screwing around with my own heuristics and not reading any papers that others were using to score higher.

    The lectures themselves were pretty interesting and well organized. I wish I spent more time just listening to them for fun, but as I said it was hard to apply most of them to the main RPM project. There were six mini coding projects that related to the course materials that were pretty interesting and not too hard. There were some written homeworks as well which were more annoying than anything, but they were fine.

    There was a lot of drama related to participation, but in my opinion it was extremely easy to get a high participation grade by just doing the reviews at the beginning of the week, requesting extra in the beginning, and posting on the forum for extra points. I got way more than the max points without even really thinking about it.

    Every assignment involved a ton of writing, but it was never really that painful to get the assignments out. A lot of people had anxiety about how to score all of the points, but if you just read what they want you'll probably get most of the points.

    The exam was kind of annoying, since it was worded confusingly, but we were allowed to use the course videos, transcripts, and Google/ChatGPT as well, so they were not too bad.

    In the end, I assume most people taking this class are doing it for the interactive intelligence specialty, and don't really care about this review. But for what it's worth, the class was decently interesting, and not too hard even though it kept me busy. I'd recommend it for someone who wants to learn Python or for someone interested in cognition and how cognition concepts are applied with code.

    Rating: 3 / 5Difficulty: 2 / 5Workload: 10 hours / week

  • jiSBPxsSUJFAZWj0X6rCyQ==December 19, 2023summer 2023

    This is not a Joyner class. This class was recorded when he was still a PhD student. KBAI lectures easily violate every single thing taught in HCI.

    Cons

    • Boring, repetitive, pointless lectures.
    • Poor assignments and projects.
    • You can hardcode through the assignments and RPM and call it 'Learning by Recording Cases'

    Pros

    • It is easy to get an A with minimal effort.
    • Coding: Using Pillow or OpenCV (for image manipulation). Implementing BFS/DFS/A* algorithms.

    Take this course if you are looking for low-effort easy A.

    Rating: 2 / 5Difficulty: 2 / 5Workload: 5 hours / week

  • YcRCrpL/0MAbjrBk0BQjiA==December 17, 2023fall 2023

    Worst class I have taken in OMSA (10 taken). Very time demanding, but not at all rigorous. Curriculum is extremely high-level and you will learn nothing that can actually be put into practice. Math for non-majors vibes. Nonetheless you will be drowning in busy work with weekly deliverables that always have a written report component and often have a code-based component as well.

    TAs are also drowning having to grade this so they just quickly check off the rubric. If you do the same, you'll get 100 even if your quality of work is terrible. Tip: always overfit the visible autograder data as you will not be penalized. You can even hardcode it - just call it case-based reasoning.

    There are homeworks, mini-projects, a major project with four milestone deliverables, exams, and a complicated engagement process all at once. None of these are well executed. Easy A it you do the assignments, but you will work hard and learn nothing. I advise strongly against taking this class.

    Rating: 1 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 18 hours / week

  • bIsKRxwXeCTRk8vgl/An2A==December 16, 2023fall 2023

    I don't have a CS or engineering background and I found this class to be really easy and will finish with a high A. The programming assignments are nothing harder than a LC medium at best and the writing assignments, while a waste of time, provide free points so long as you follow the rubric.

    I would note that the role of TAs in this course is not great. Here, the TAs are there for operational support, not to clarify questions about lecture content. This is pretty annoying since the lectures are very dated with poor audio and incorrect/incoherent captions. However, outside of name dropping a few lecture concepts here and there for the writing assignments, they aren't really needed since the exams are open book.

    The class average on the exams were around an 80%. I think this is heavily skewed to the left due to some really bad performers. Do note, that some of the exam questions were very poorly worded. This led to a lot of non-native English speakers to struggle.

    Rating: 3 / 5Difficulty: 1 / 5Workload: 8 hours / week

  • ZexEWQ7ZxYse5rRX3UGGsw==December 15, 2023fall 2023

    The content is interesting from a historical perspective and to add flavor and depth to some of the challenges still currently facing modern connectionist AI (e.g. commonsense reasoning, planning, logic). It seems unlikely that these techniques will prove the key to AGI, but you never know, and having more tools in one's toolset is always a good thing.

    This semester had a clusterfuck around peer review, Dr. Goel was largely absent from participation, and the TA staff was okay at best.

    You also need to follow the rubrics to the letter to get a good grade. But once you do that, the rest is up to you. This class epitomizes the concept of you get out what you put in.

    You can sleep through the class and not apply a single concept from the lectures and brute force solve the mini-projects and use trivial heuristics to solve RPM and you can still get an A. Or you can spend some time digesting the lectures and reading the extra material to really feel the sense of where these topics can go.

    I enjoyed the material a lot, so the class was a net positive for me.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 2 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • M1tKWAQmDe/GVdhtZdjGSg==December 14, 2023fall 2023

    I can't recommend this class. This is my 9th class and it's one of the worst. I took HCI from Joyner and it was amazing, I thought this would be similar and it's complete polar opposite.

    I think you can see what this class use to be like and the elements are still there, but while the class has been updated, it hasn't culled away any of the content in 3+ years. It really is a constant barrage of assignments and things to do and its every single week. There is no break. Not even for Thanksgiving or for GT's fall break, and I know it's online but every Fall class I have taken before this gave some sort of breathing room.

    This is the standard week for this class: 2-3 hour lecture videos. 3 peer review (which is graded by a computer and they swear it's not based on word count but it is), office hours, a coding assignment (some really small but most are very involved 300+ lines) and a 5-page paper explaining your code. And to top it off you have to participate in the discussion board to receive participation points.

    Additionally, you will get nickel and dimed on points for the rubric that the TA won't follow. I seriously had multiple custom deductions on assignments which at that point why even use a rubric. You can submit a regrade request but it's not as easy as just requesting it, you have to submit a doctoral thesis with a 3 page paper explaining why you got points taken off (and they swear that the TAs are not subjective, but again, they are)

    The project is also terrible. In theory it sounds like a good idea, but the project literally comes down to more graphic analysis then it does AI. For the most part, everyone has the same idea of how to do things, but everyone's process is different. The exemplary examples they have are chosen based on what how well put together the paper looks but it's just for looks. Looking at some of the examples for help doesn't work because there are so many errors on them that you could find yourself coding in circles.

    TLDR: Skip this class unless you have a lot of free time on your hands and even then I would skip. Unless they cull away some of the fat and let the students breathe for a little bit this class is not worth it.

    Rating: 1 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 18 hours / week

  • /hHeqIE4kcRkbYKegTGfKg==December 13, 2023fall 2023

    Grade: currently right on A/B border, might get a B.

    Lectures: usually interesting, sometimes slow with the examples they walk through. The lectures would really benefit from some good code examples. Everything is presented very abstractly and translating what they present into actual usable code is a pretty huge challenge most of the time.

    Mini projects: usually not too bad, I usually solved these with algorithms I didn't learn in the course, because they don't really present many algorithms, mostly just abstract framework.

    Ravens Project: Very hard, I thought this was an interesting project to think through, but they don't give you any magical easy way to approach it in the lectures. I learned a lot about opencv/image processing. Get started early, I kept expecting the lectures to give me some a ha moment on how to approach these problems, but there is no easy solution (except farming gradescope which some students figured out how to do and shared it on the forum, pretty disappointed about this actually, they said they will fix this issue next semester). To get an A you are probably going to end up with a brute force agent with miles of if statements, unless you think up something clever, I ended up using a nearest neighbor approach which wasn't bad to code and worked well enough to get a 75/96.

    Tests: Awful, open everything, but trying to find jargon specific to the class which you can't find in the text book and it might just be stated briefly in a lecture video. Best way to study I think is to just watch the lectures over and over and take good notes.

    This class feels hard for the wrong reasons: -getting nitpicked on writing assignment points, -tests that don't really reflect actual problem solving skills, mostly just understanding jargon.

    • ravens project is really hard and a fun challenge, but if some students figure out a gradescope farm you will be working your ass of while they get perfect scores for a hour or two of work (I didn't do this technique because it seemed like a complete waste of my time, not learning anything by doing this) -10% participation grade which is no cake walk to get, need to reliably do peer reviews by Tuesdays and comment on the forum a lot.

    Rating: 3 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 12 hours / week

  • CJsHkG9iRi7KVP3msEBxlg==December 13, 2023fall 2023

    Lectures: Interesting concepts in classical AI. Well-presented materials.

    Assignments: The assignments are similar in format to Harvard's CS50 AI course, but more challenging (No starter code is provided, but you have the freedom to choose any methods you prefer). Overall, they were interesting and well-connected to the lectures (if you choose to use the methods introduced in the lectures).

    Final project: 70% of my time was spent on the final project because I really enjoyed the project. Most people spent much less time and still did well.

    Grading: Depends on your luck. Different TAs have different expectations. If you are assigned to a TA who interprets the rubric differently, you will get points deducted. This was what made this course stressful to a lot of people (You will find your heart beating at 150bpm whenever they release the grades).

    Overall, this is a well-designed course for someone new to classical AI. The course was enjoyable but I would recommend against taking the class because you can just watch the lecture videos online and do the assignments yourself. This would save you from unnecessary stress. (I give it a rating of 1 not because the quality was bad, but because I think there is really no need to enroll in the class.)

    Rating: 1 / 5Difficulty: 1 / 5Workload: 22 hours / week

  • 2eEQQot0U7f1Fk2pKkCFxg==December 8, 2023fall 2023

    This is an easy class if you have already taken AI. I even think you probably don't need to take it at all if you are in the ML track. If you're looking for a side class accompanying some big classes, or just looking to graduate, I would recommend it. Otherwise, I'd say you'll learn nothing.

    Some tips:

    1. For the RPM project, you have 3 milestones to work towards before submitting the final report. For each milestone performance score, even you could get 100% grade by solving half of the test problems, I still strongly encourage you to get them all if possible. That will save you a lot of effort on the final stage.
    2. Also for the RPM, make sure you come up with a way to effectively determine if two images are the same in the early milestones. Some images look the same, but in pixel-by-pixel they vary. This semester we have observed students using several ways, but none of them seem to work neatly. Most students are using a threshold.
    3. For each writings, just refer to the rubric. The rubric is comprehensive and clear. And that's the best part of this class.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 2 / 5Workload: 4 hours / week

  • C/BaLnCv4w7JOQXmBFKvhw==December 5, 2023fall 2023

    I agree with all the reviews posted so far from the Fall 2023 class. Although previous semesters had some positive and some negative reviews, I'm not surprised to see only negative reviews from this semester.

    Pros: Assignments are easy to understand. Assignments can be worked on ahead of time. This course was really flexible in terms of scheduling. Work load is dispersed quite evenly throughout the semester (some of the final assignments are easier but at that point you should be working on your final project)

    Cons: Grading can be quite subjective due to the nature of essays. The course videos appear related to the assignments, but they are related in superficial ways. Two snafus during the course lead to large disruptions in learning.

    This class really needs the help of an involved professor. The lack of an involved professor made this course seem like a circus.

    Rating: 2 / 5Difficulty: 2 / 5Workload: 12 hours / week

  • paO+vEvs0e9ZVK4/2VgLog==December 2, 2023fall 2023

    KBAI Review

    TL;DR I am a Mech Eng and Software Eng and I learned absolutely nothing worth anything in this course. You basically spend the whole semester solving problems each week using whatever algorithm you want but call it an “Agent”. The material is outdated and I am shocked that this is allowed in the curriculum of a top 10 graduate CS program.

    I’d recommend for - students who have no/little software background and want an easy start and can tolerate material that will likely be irrelavent if you want to do anything with AI as well as students who just want an easy A to get MS credentials. Or if you like structured learning environments and want to pay to practice solving problems that you could just do on your own.

    I’d NOT recommend for - students who genuinely want to learn something relavent in the AI space and can’t tolerate wasting your time having to memorize and regurgitate outdated information so you can pass exams.

    ———DETAILED RATINGS----

    Organization: Excellent The organization and responsiveness of the TAs is excellent. At no point in the course was I stressed out because I awas unclear about something or left wondering for days/weeks. When tweaks were made, they were clearly communicated. (Unfortunately, however, you only have to get a B in a course to be a TA so many of them are not particularly knowledgeable. They are basically just glorified grading tools. Don’t ask them a sophisticated question about….idk equivalent problem representations…. or they will break and take points away for non-conformity lmao)

    Grading: Fair There was nothing unreasonable or unexpected grading-wise if you know how to read the rubric and check the boxes.

    Workload: Way too much busy work I probably work a bit slower than average and with the exception of the first assignment or two everything I did for this class felt like torturous busy work. I think to give us a break whey made the last few assignments easier and maybe some people appreciated that but I felt it was just more mindless work…I’d have preferred an actual dropped assignment or something so I can focus on research for a week and forget about class work.

    (Note - I can’t say anything about the workload compared to other OMSCS courses as this is my first one)

    Material: Outdated and irrelevant in the AI space This was the main frustration for me in this course. I do not care (more than I have to for survival) about social credibilitly. I care about learning and my efforts and research helping the world. This classes wasted 4 months of my time (hyperbole acknowledged) solving weekly problems I don’t care about and watching 25 lectures that made me want to break my monitors. Whats hilarious is that if you used the methods we “learned” in class on the assignments, it would have made the assignments harder to do then just using simple heuristics and search algs.

    A much more appropriate name for this course would be "Ancient AI", "Applied Algorithms", "Narrow AI", “Techniques for Dumb Robots”, ...¯_(ツ)_/¯

    There are two intellectually distasteful things in this class for me 1) you have to learn the KBAI rebranding of existing concepts you already learn far more broadly and efficiently in other classes (ie. algorithms and data structures) and 2) the whole class is about various static algorithms that people have come up with in the past to solve AI problems that have produce terriblly narrow and non-extensible AI systems like those terrible phone robots that you have to get past to talk to real people about your problem. Sure, these systems work for some narrow applications but they are just static algorithms, not AI. Or like your Roomba….basically algorithms for dumb, narrow “AI".

    There are enough actually challanging problems in the world that require understanding complex processes and so in a top tier program making students waste their time memorizing KBAI's rebranding of things they a) already know by another name or b) can learn in algorithms classes more efficiently is truly yuck.

    In short, this class is a waste of brain space because its basically a rebranding of things you should already know if you take, math and other fundamental CS courses. If you want cognition, take a neuroscience or another course. Not this. Otherwise just math, algorithms, and a minimal degree of introspection regarding how your own brain works is all you need to know everything in this class.

    One additional TIP because I like a level playing field for those who still decide to take this class after my glowing review: I (foolishly) completed my final project using IPR/DPR and a few little image processing techniques….but come to find out you can get 100% by just hardcoding all of the solutions and calling it "learning by recording cases” even though YOU are the one learning…not your socalled “agent". facepalm

    ———

    Context: Background: Mech Eng and Software Eng Fall’23 - KBAI: A

    Rating: 1 / 5Difficulty: 1 / 5Workload: 16 hours / week

  • Jp6LPDDpZQ6O6BKJyCgsmw==December 1, 2023fall 2023

    This was a strange course. At first, I was excited for the challenge and learning something far different than anything else in OMSA. But I've had several issues with the course. It's a very "busy" course - frankly just too much.

    Many of the assignments seem to provide little learning value for me. Many of the mini-projects were a form of space-searches. The RPM project also seems promising, and I'd like to have focused more on it, but I just got a little bit burnt-out on the amount of coursework. I think people typically will say "deal with it, you're getting a GaTech degree, etc..." but in reality, less is more. People only have so much bandwidth, and additional assignments are diminishing (if not negative) returns.
