    Outside of this, the course had a weird focus on catching cheaters. Much of the class was accused of cheating at one point on peer reviews (strangely). The teaching staff continued to double-down on this accusation, until they realized their errors, and retracted it. It was a very strange event overall.

    Not sure I would recommend this course without significant modifications.

    Rating: 2 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 16 hours / week

  • n70rALB3Re1J2WuKho9B4A==November 17, 2023fall 2023

    Avoid this class if possible. So MUCH tedious and useless work. I honestly have learned little about AI. My writing probably has improved, after having to write a journal for every single coding assignment and every milestone of the semester long project. The midterm was like a reading comprehension exam with confusing words.

    Rating: 2 / 5Difficulty: 2 / 5Workload: 12 hours / week

  • zShfZg8vVcLSfUgNVJeG1A==November 14, 2023fall 2023

    There is no AI in this course. All the randomly selected puzzles are to be solved by 'so-called' AI agents. There are many Mini-projects, Homework, and MileStones. For each assignment, you have to write ~4-5 pages of report (weekly), and that is too much for a course that is not AI. Each assignment is different and shallow in terms of learning. The course lectures are outdated and not useful nowadays. The grading is done by AI mostly, and I did not see a single comment on my submissions. The course will take a lot of time, and you will gain almost zero knowledge about AI. It is good for high-school kids, but not for MSc students in CS! I forgot to say that participation is mandatory and you have to write peer-review for others' assignments. This feedback takes so much time, and they are useless. I would call it 'abusing' student to assess submissions and grading based on their feedback reports (may save costs!). 10% for participation is insane, and there is no fun there.

    Rating: 1 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 20 hours / week

  • ViQ0MAhqIk8zZadGZ6QAWw==October 5, 2023fall 2023

    I would only recommend taking this as your first course in OMSCS. Taking this in my last semester makes the tedious projects and boring lectures mind-numbing. There are non-stop peer review assignments, an unnecessary amount of papers and generally uninteresting content. I've worked way harder in classes like ML, RL or DL and had a blast. AI4R and Game AI are similar in workload and far more interesting.

    Rating: 2 / 5Difficulty: 2 / 5Workload: 10 hours / week

  • A+tPab0Hk4DbA+nSHoXpJQ==September 24, 2023fall 2023

    I'm not a fan of the peer feedback thing - I came here to write this. I hope my grade isn't dependant on the other students.

    I wish lectures were more relevant to the assignments.

    I'm not learning anything that will be used outside of this course (and if I will, I've already learned it in undergrad) - and I work in reasearch, I think it's a more specific Data Structures/Algorithms course.

    This class is busy work and basic problem solving that is beng used for publications. It's underwater basket weaving - but you don't have goggles and assignments are due every week.

    Rating: 2 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 12 hours / week

  • cf3D+u/Iv7R358u1LGSccQ==August 21, 2023summer 2023

    This was my second semester and I picked HCI in the first sem. I received an A in this course over the Summer of 2023.

    Lecture Videos: The lecture videos were very abstract and left a room for a lot of thinking. I wish I could have the time during the summer to think about these abstract topics and apply these concepts in the Mini Projects and RPM Projects. But summer being very condensed it was not at all possible to do this. Most of my mini-projects were brute force approaches to solve the problem.

    If you really want to apply these concepts, I would recommend a spring / fall semester as it is not possible in the Summer. I had to work 1-2 weeks ahead to keep up with the pace of the course.

    Assignments: The course has 3 different types of assignment running parallel: The Mini Projects, the Homeworks and the RPM project. All the assignments were easy with well layed out rubrics which helps to answer the questions. I lost marks mostly in Homework 2 and 3 as a lot of room to answer was left upon us Students, the lectures did not help much here.

    Exams: Exams were open book and easy. I have no complaints.

    Overall: I came in with little to no Python knowledge and was easily able to pick up the materials and RPM project on the go with lots of Google Searches, etc. This course is an easy course, I learnt a lot about Knowledge Based AI but I am not happy that I could not apply the concepts I wanted to for the RPM projects. I look forward to do this during my downtime.

    I wish they could condense the course in the Summer so we can apply the concepts learnt and not rush through the semester. I would recommend picking this during Fall/Spring as unlike other courses, no assignments/homeworks have been dropped.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 19 hours / week

  • 2mbR2IFqzmJyu68V3CjyZw==August 13, 2023summer 2023

    It is my 1st course in OMSCS. And I regret deeply for didn't take the time to check out reviews here and watch some videos of this useless course before paying for it. My opinions:

    1. videos are very useless, taking about concepts in a way like a daily conversation anyone can come up in school. In the end, teach students bascially nothing if not to take exams 1 & 2.
    2. too much writing, feel like a course for MBA student. too much writing for homework, min-project, RPM. too time consuming.
    3. Joyner's file format is a joke. Joyner is also a terribel teacher, trying to connect everything in the lecture to RPM, which makes zero sense.
    4. serious questions questioning lecture videos/notes don't get answers from teaching team. I suppose they trap themselves with poorly prepared notes and videos.

    Overall, just don't take this one unless you are required to do so.

    Rating: 1 / 5Difficulty: 1 / 5Workload: 8 hours / week

  • aPUo+fai3Uca5eV1JXAiNw==August 7, 2023summer 2023

    Overall, I didn't learn as much as I wanted to in this course. It was alright, but not what I hoped for. Most projects could be brute forced, a lot of writing that seemed fairly pointless, Joyner Peer Feedback which was almost always useless forms of "good job" from fellow classmates. I will say I really appreciated everything being unlocked right away, I powered through lectures and assignments usually being 3-4 weeks ahead of schedule on work. Taking this class in the summer was pretty manageable. I got basically 100 on all mini projects and homeworks. 94/110 on Exam 1 and 92/110 on Exam 2 and 64/96 for the semester long final project with 50/50 on the final project writeup. This secured a solid A for me. If you know python, you are probably fine for the class, as you see even a meh score on the final project doesn't drag you down too much. Just stay up to date on the schedule (or work ahead). I wish there was slightly more challenging homework that really required application of AI rather than brute force algorithms that you pitch as AI in your reports. Literally with any approach you take, just follow the rubric and drop some keywords and you score full points. The Artificial Intelligence course in OMSCS is much harder but much more informative in terms of AI techniques and approaches. I expected more from this class, but oh well, it was an easy enough A for me.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 8 hours / week

  • C17orAyBtVbxMT3SftseFw==August 6, 2023summer 2023

    I'm reviewing this class after receiving an "A" grade for the Summer 2023 term. Overall I had a positive experience with this course.

    • This class could very easily be renamed "Applied Computer Vision" or "Applied Algorithms" considering the main RPM project, and mini-projects, respectively. The lectures do not go into any detail about OpenCV / Pillow / NumPy, so expect a very "hands-on" style deep-dive into these topics. The class also expects a decent familiarity with Python and about good programming principles in general.

    • You probably shouldn't take this course during the summer. While it's possible to get an A, it will probably interfere with your life more than you want it to, and you will not have as much time to grasp the concepts in the lectures. Many of the assignments will feel rushed, and there are some weeks where both a homework / mini-project / exam is due the same week as as an RPM milestone; this is not ideal.

    • Dr. Joyner, as usual, does an amazing job at administering the course, and allows students many opportunities for grade improvement. As is usual with his courses, expect the extra work of giving Peer Feedback every week. You will also find yourself looking on the EdStem forums a lot for this course, not only for the extra participation threads, but also for the assignments themselves (more on that later).

    • The weakest point of this class is the lectures. Dr. Ashok Goel was the initial creator of the course, and Dr. Joyner is a co-host of the lectures. The lecture material does not really help while doing the assignments. Most of the material is very conceptual, and only a few of the lectures series have a real, technical description of an algorithm you should use on an assignment. I found this disappointing because the lectures became a chore every week. Instead of learning the material I would later apply in an assignment, it felt like I was learning the theory for the sake of learning the theory.

    My recommendation to the course maintainers was for them to drop somewhere between 33 - 40% of the lecture videos, and with that remaining space, create new lecture videos which focus on implementation. The course covers so many topics that it does feel a bit overwhelming. Those videos in the 33 - 40% felt to me like topics which I only needed to know for the exams, and nothing else. It would be nice if the course could give some basic ideas / tutorials on computer vision fundamentals, and about how actually useful parts of the course material can be implemented in code.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 20 hours / week

  • QOt+OECo7JhCuBoS7jz0nA==August 3, 2023summer 2023

    This was a great course. the content is well organized and Dr. Joyner goes above and beyond on helping the students stay on track by sharing and posting on ed lessons the week announcements.

    The concepts of the class are not hard to understand but do require you to pay attention and think about them. The interaction on the forum was really good and it really helps to figure out details on the projects.

    There is a primary project that you build during the semester and 5 mini projects. All the material is available on day 1 so you can work ahead but there are weekly discussions on the topics assigned for the current week.

    All of the projects can be built based on the material from the class, at least conceptually. in practice, you will need some knowledge of Python but nothing that you can't learn as you take the course.

    The main project involves image processing but is very light and you could even go ahead without it. PIL and OpenCV will be your friends.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 16 hours / week

  • +rlO22mv1WfHSZ5PuZEMhQ==August 2, 2023summer 2023

    Overall, this is a good course. The coding assignments are fun and they’re not too tough if you have any Python experience. The exams are open note and all multiple-selection T/F type questions, so it’s not too stressful. The material is pretty basic. So, if this is an early course in the program, you’ll get eased into some material. But if it’s later course, you might be a little bored.

    Biggest downside is that there’s a LOT of writing. Way too much writing. Writing about your code. Shorter writing prompts based on course material. Write about other student’s assignments. Just way too much writing. If you can’t pump out 5-8 pages of writing easily, you’re going to have a bad time.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 2 / 5Workload: 18 hours / week

  • zTJ8T+Gj/4Ug02bYkrIqGw==August 1, 2023spring 2023

    This class was not very difficult. What I liked is that Dr. Joyner opened up all the assignments (even the midterm/final exam) at the beginning of the semester. This allows students to get a head start on assignments. I highly recommend to get a good 1 assignment ahead so you don't need to get stressed out.

    There is a new assignment to do every week, so as long as you've got reasonable time management skills, you'll do fine.

    In addition, get ALL the participation points you can, it can easily drag down your grade if you slack off on doing it.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • wL7yMD5D3KVB3wA+m+xXlA==June 12, 2023spring 2023

    David Joiner & Ashok Goel are great instructors for the class! The workload isn't too bad and there's no group projects (thank god, lol)

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 13 hours / week

  • CU+6kBgOqN1KD7ZDImMi9A==June 7, 2023spring 2023

    This is an amazing introductory course in the program that I wish I had taken as my first OMSCS course. Lectures are engaging and easy to understand. I paired this class with AIES and was easily able to get an A. Good mix of programming and writing.

    I went into this course with zero "AI" experience and was surprised at how digestable and easily understandable everything was. The hardest portion of this class is the semester long project but I thought it was a really fun assignment.

    I understand that writing the papers can be annoying, but they are actually really simple to write and easy As (as long as you follow the syllabus).

    Dr. Joyner is so good at crafting these courses that are challenging and engaging while also fostering a sense of community through Ed Discussion. He actually cares whether you learn something from a course which I can't say about other courses I've taken in OMSCS (AIES, Global Entrepreneurship).

    Don't be intimidated by the title! If I can do it, I promise you can

    Pros:

    • The programming assignments are somewhat challenging but extremely doable. The lectures give you everything you need to solve the projects. If you're stuck you can also check Ed Discussion where someone will definitely have posted a high-level description of the solution (this helped me so much!)

    • Lectures are really engaging (as long as you haven't fallen behind and are binging them)

    • Helpful student engagement and discussion in Ed Discussion

    Cons:

    • While I really enjoyed the semster long project, it kind of felt like there was the "one way" to do it with IPR/DPR. I know that's not true, but it really felt that way. I wish the lectures and the RPM problems were closer to each other.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 12 hours / week

  • JAxCrWgIR8adOXDV05M+tA==May 17, 2023spring 2023

    Background: BS degree in civil engineering with ~2 years of development experience. KBAI was my second course in the program after HCI.

    As mentioned in this course, there are basically four schools of AI: designing agents that (1) think optimally, (2) act optimally, (3) think like humans, or (4) act like humans. Knowledge-Based Artificial Intelligence is focused on (3) - how can we design AI agents that think like humans? With that in mind, this course has significant overlap with cognitive science and philosophy in an effort to understand how humans think. The lectures fly at a pretty high level, but I found them interesting and thought-provoking. Many folks mention the writing assignments as a drawback to this course, but it's to be expected in a Joyner course and I think it's perfectly reasonable for graduate-level coursework. A lot of the writing is focused on communicating your problem solving approach for homework assignments, which I consider to be an essential skill. It doesn't really matter how slick your algorithms are if you can't explain to anyone how they work.

    Overall, I would recommend taking KBAI. I didn't have any prior experience with AI, so this was a nice introduction to the field. You'll definitely improve your Python skills, and you'll get your feet wet with rudimentary NLP and computer vision. I really enjoyed the semester-long RPM project because I got to learn more about NumPy and OpenCV.

    With that said, I will agree with a prior comment about how KBAI feels like the "underwater basket weaving" of AI. Throughout the semester I couldn't quite get past the fact that it all felt too high level and simplistic. It was a very unique time to take this course because it happened smack in the middle of the ChatGPT explosion, and so I couldn't escape the feeling that the little logic problems presented in the assignments fall short of what "real AI" looks like. If you're looking to dive deep into algorithms, AI or ML might be a better choice.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 13 hours / week

  • ysijcFNZ+u0A9GPN24ntOg==May 10, 2023spring 2023

    I thoroughly enjoyed how KBAI combines writing and programming to create a well-informed understanding of AI at a high level. The course is structured around three major assignment types: Mini-Projects that leverage a particular AI strategy to solve programming problems in Python; written assignments that require analysis of AI topics and their application to real-world scenarios; and a semester-long project on using Python to engage in image transformation detection for solving Raven's Progressive Matrices. These assignments rotated weekly, which I felt was helpful in ensuring that I didn't get burnt out on a particular topic.

    I found myself extremely overwhelmed by KBAI at the start of the course but eventually found a groove that made the workload manageable. The first Mini-Project was significantly more difficult for me to complete than any other assignment - I ended up with a 50-something on GradeScope and seriously contemplated withdrawing from the course only 2 or 3 weeks in. That said, once I better understood the requirements for each of the Mini-Projects and major project milestones, the work became almost trivial. Prior knowledge of Python is a major plus that will help you significantly throughout this course.

    Many reviewers of this course have branded its lecture videos as a distraction from the actual course content, and generally I tend to agree. The lectures cover various problem-solving and knowledge topics at a high level but never provide details on how they could be implemented in a programming environment; I found some lectures to be more useful than others in terms of guiding me toward solutions. Knowledge of the lecture video content is only truly necessary for the written Homework assignments and the 2 exams.

    The real secret to success in this course lies in community participation: go-getter students are very eager to share how they completed particular assignments or problems, allowing you to skip over the process of deliberating how to solve such problems and instead focus on implementation. I highly recommend lurking around Ed if you are stuck on something.

    This course rewards those who are either: a) willing to dedicate significant hours to coming up with original solutions; or b) clever at making most of the resources offered to them and not obsessing over a perfect score. You can easily maintain a high grade by being deliberate about which assignments you're capable of getting a 100 on and letting these pad some of your lower-performing code submissions. I finished with a mid-90s final grade despite earning a 76/100 on my final project's performance.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 2 / 5Workload: 12 hours / week

  • 71mtit++6oWQpVDyILLbjA==May 9, 2023spring 2023

    Background: This is my first course in the OMSCS program. I have a BS in Computer Engineering and most of my programming experience is in C#/.NET. Took a 5-6 year break from school and currently work as a Software Developer professionally.

    Overall: This course is a great introductory course for those who took a long break from undergrad or for those who want to get better at programming without taking a deep dive into highly complex topics. All the mini-projects and main project require you to use Python. From my personal experience, I took most of the time (for the first month) trying to understand how to set up Python and getting used to the differences in syntax compared to C#. Also, an initial lack of knowledge in NumPy, PIL, and OpenCV libraries, which are essential for the Final project, significantly increased development time for the Project Milestones. Although the Homeworks are basically long essays, I found them to be the least time consuming assignments of the course. I struggled in this course initially because a lot of the mini-projects required knowledge in different data structures, which I lacked, and had to play catch up to eventually get an A in the course. Mini-Project 2 and 3 were the most difficult assignments, based on my experience, so do not get discouraged if you score low on those.

    Tips: • Use the course forum if you feel like you are struggling in the programming assignments. (The other students in the course are very helpful and give great advice) • Try to write additional peer feedbacks if you don't plan to be active in the forum. (I found I had to play catch up towards the end of the semester for my participation points) • Play around with the Python libraries that are used for the RPM project when you have free time. (It will help lower development time for your project) • Focus on the rubric for the assignments rather than longevity.

    What I got out of the course: • I improved on my Python programming • Learned different ways to approach problems • Better understanding of the importance of planning • Better awareness of time requirements for a Graduate course

    I would definitely recommend this course as an introductory course for the OMSCS program based on the overall good experience I had.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 14 hours / week

  • Fx764/8uQ176zln00wiLog==May 1, 2023spring 2023

    I really enjoyed this class, I love a Joyner class in general. Lecture material is interesting and the projects are all just the right amount of hard. You have to think and put in effort but none took more than maybe 5 hours for the week. There is a paper due with every project, but they take no time to write up and do not have to be anything crazy to get full points. Feel like I learned a lot and wasn't stressed over this class. I really looked forward to working on the course project (there are weeks set aside just to work on the overall project).

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 2 / 5Workload: 8 hours / week

  • SBM5hSPk0ab6sFPaDrTVUQ==April 30, 2023spring 2023

    My first course in the OMSCS program, my background is BS in mechanical engineering and I have been a working professional for 8 years. My CS background is limited, and my work does not use python. With that being said, a great first course! I enjoyed the jumpstart into programming in python, and the class has a great discussion forum that is very lively. I had to get used to the flow, you typically have to write ~4 pages a week on homework, and have to work on creating a working code that is submitted to be graded. The beginning of the course did require a lot of work, the first coding project took me 15 hours to complete but only because my programming was rusty and needed to get up to speed. After that initial kick I got the hang of it, and really enjoyed how goofy some of the project was. I will say after each exam I felt like I didn't learn much about KBAI as some of the definitions were hard to pick up from the lessons. This course to me was more about the enjoyment I got from the homework, mini-projects, and final. Highly recommend to anyone who is in a similar boat and wants to dive in. Be warned, I did say 13 hours a week, but in the beginning I did have some 20-25 hour weeks, but towards the end I had some 4-5 hour weeks to balance it out. And yes I am a nerd and kept track of all my time, the actual average was 12.3 hours, but decided to round up to be on the safe side if someone is trying to plan out a semester workload.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 13 hours / week

  • tTb8KqAw9Zs01vBDk45q4A==April 28, 2023spring 2023

    I love this class! It's my first class and a great transition to the program. The projects are so much fun and really improve my coding skills. Students on the forum are always there to share ideas and tips and help each other. It's surprising how friendly the classroom environment is despite being online. The TAs who post on the forum are nice and professional, even when people post angry complaints.

    The biggest complaint I've seen is the writing. There's writing every week. Personally, I don't think it's that bad since the required JDF format has big margins. Just make sure you hit the rubric and be clear with your thoughts.

    Although the lectures focus more on the theories, which seems to be a masters-level thing, you can tell the professors put in a lot of thoughts to incorporate the materials in the assignments.

    The RPM project is my favorite part of the class. It's so rewarding to see it come together by the end of the semester. The discussions on the forum on various approaches and testing make it feel like you are working as a team.

    Everything's available at the start of the semester which is really nice for working ahead. Lastly, this class is very organized as it's one of Dr. Joyner's class. It just makes your life so much easier when everything's clear.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 12 hours / week

  • v4LR6S9MkbR3pgWZGbgr7Q==April 12, 2023spring 2023

    I just finished the final exam. It is open book/note/lecture/everything, but I realized how little I learned in this class.

    Pros: Everything is released right away. All assignments are online, so you can get started whenever you want (barring those assignments changing for the next semester).

    Professor is active in the forums but that should be the norm, not a pro.

    Lots of graded assignments to cushion the blow if you do poorly on something.

    Cons: Lectures are just high level and hand wavy. Examples that are abstract to the point of being absurd. "Suppose a robot wants to buy a cup of coffee". That stuff is just laughable. This content has nothing to do with the mini-projects or RPM. MPs can almost be solved with a search algorithm (BFS), other MPs you can just hack something together.

    You can get 70% performance on RPM with affine model, DPR/IPR hacks and a look up table. No reason to go beyond that since you'll lose only a couple points on your final grade.

    80% is the average success rate for students on RPM performance, rarely anyone gets 100%. So your grade is penalized by virtue of tackling the problem. 80% performance should be 100% of that part of the grade.

    Some of the homework questions were tangential and not with-in the scope of the class. But it is easy busy work to pad your grade, so maybe a pro.

    Cognitive Science is just pseudo-philosophy. Unfortunately, this class is just that with a sprinkling of AI and not the other way around. KBAI feels like the underwater basket weaving of AI. Maybe interesting to think about (if that is your thing) but completely useless in the real world.

    My $0.02:

    This class failed since it didn't teach me anything. That is what I learned on the final exam. Just topics that you watch a video for, take some notes and you'll probably forget a week or two after the semester is done. I wish this class showed some actual application to the real world. How does KBAI tie into Chat GPT? Does Boston Dynamics implement KBAI into their robots? Those kinds of things.

    I recommend checking out the course page and see if this is something of interest before you register. It is an easy class to get a B and fulfill the Interactive Intelligence specialization requirement, but kind of a waste of time when you could be learning something useful or that would apply to your future career. I feel like I left AI having learned new skills but I leave KBAI wondering if I should have just taken ML instead.

    Rating: 2 / 5Difficulty: 2 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • IJogf93Efwuq4RZeTrroVA==April 12, 2023spring 2023

    Pros: -The videos of this class are absolutely the best. They're short and sweet but you'll also learn a lot from them, I felt they're changing the way I think. -Exams are reasonable, not too hard, not too difficult for someone watched all the lectures. Cons: Pretty much everything else :) -Every single week you have to write a journal, but the issue isn't in writing them as much as how they get graded. During the whole semester, so many students complained on ED that they didn't get graded fairly which is kind of true, your grade depends on how nice the TA is and they're all subjective so it's hard to argue against it. For some reason the professor didn't get engage in any of those threads. -TAs are there to review those journals, they're rarely engaged in anything would truly help the students. -The projects are silly and you don't need AI to solve them, they're all solvable through brute force, in fact, I haven't seen anyone else in the class solving them in a different way based on the journals which I had to review during the peer review process.

    Long story short, take that class if you don't have other options, otherwise, watch the videos of the class on your own, they're available online for everyone.

    Rating: 2 / 5Difficulty: 2 / 5Workload: 8 hours / week

  • v4LR6S9MkbR3pgWZGbgr7Q==March 29, 2023spring 2023

    I have to rant about KBAI to someone or I will lose my mind

    I had to write a paper about what a sandwich is. How to play UNO using if-then statements. What free will is. What a joke is.

    This class is a joke. Every homework is just busy work. Here's a great line from the prompt for home work 3:

    As we all know, our appreciation for literature is only increased when we painstakingly tear it apart and analyze it like a chemical compound, so let’s do that here—and save pondering how to design an AI agent that can understand the sarcasm of that sentence for another day.

    No! Professor, are you serious? No! I am in a Comp Sci program. I am a professional with a life. This isn't funny. This is you looking your student in the face and saying "im making you do unrelated busy work instead of what you came here for, and I'm going to laugh at you about it." This is every homework.

    I hate this class. It makes the whole OMSCS program feel like a bad joke of an online school. I literally lost points today because a TA didn't like how I defined what a hotdog is. Are you kidding me? In a masters level grad program about computer science?

    The first thing I ever programmed on my own was an if-then stupid little program to play blackjack. Turns out that was actually masters level work because Joyner was happy to assign an UNO if-then stupid little program as homework (and then call it an "AI agent" lol). This class is a literal insult to the intelligence of its students.

    But at least we get cool AI based homeworks right?

    NO. NO, YOU REALLY DONT.

    For every assignment you can just brute force your way to perfect score, and what's more, that's usually the best way to do it. The lectures are full of stuff about AI that only gets applied when students drop buzzwords from lectures to try to satisfy TAs who want to see us "apply what we learned". In other words, replacing the word "class" with "frame" and "variable" with "slot". Boom, you can do AI now.

    The TAs for this class have no business deciding if I defined free will sufficiently at a masters level. I have no business trying to do so at a masters level. I'm not here for philosophy, I super don't care what Joyner or his TAs feel about free will. Like, at all. And if they care what I think then they're even more bonkers than I thought. What even is this class?

    I don't know. I'm never taking another Joyner class again.

    Rating: 1 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 20 hours / week

  • vmiB/s7inuXS4HtZdFa1lQ==March 22, 2023spring 2023

    This was my first course in the program. I feel comfortable writing this review six weeks before the end of the semester as I have fully explored all the course content, submitted all my code, and submitted almost all of written work. I currently have a 98 in the course and fully expect to maintain an ‘A’. This is a relatively easy course if you have a background in CS, but make no mistake, it is time-consuming. I was only able to work ahead because I have significantly more time than the average student to put toward schoolwork.

    PROS: -- The content of the course is interesting. It feels like a cross between an intro to AI and an intro to cognitive science. I learned a lot from the different concepts covered in the lectures, from semantic networks, to incremental concept learning, to analogical reasoning. If you’re interested in the nature of intelligence and minds/possible minds, you’ll probably find this stuff fascinating. -- The lectures are well produced and easy to consume. I think this is the case with most courses in the program, but like I said, this is my first course, so I really appreciated it. -- Because the course is popular and has such a high student capacity, Ed was always very active. Every question asked was answered quickly and satisfactorily by a fellow student or a TA. Communicating with others on Ed was actually one of my favorite parts of the course. I learned a lot just from these interactions. -- David Joyner. I just love the guy. -- The programming was pretty fun. If you’re looking for something on the easier side, are just getting started in the program, or don’t have much background in CS, the projects are a good way to get some exercise with python and maybe a little numpy. -- The exams are fair and very well put together. No gotchas, no ambiguity (that I saw), and the difficult level was appropriate for an open-note, open-internet format–challenging but not too hard. That said, I think I would have preferred a somewhat easier, closed-note exam format. I personally learn more that way.

    CONS: -- I’m not sure how directly useful the course content is in industry. This isn’t a euphemism; I’m honestly not sure. Judging from what others have said (I’m not in tech professionally yet), these concepts probably fall on the lower end of practicality. However, I still think they’re useful at a high level when considering what the properties of a truly intelligent agent are. -- The programming assignments, including the central Raven’s Progressive Matrices project, don’t require you to use the course material and thus end up not complementing the course the way they should. To me, this is the biggest flaw of the class. Your program results are auto-graded, so as long as your output gets you across the finish line, whatever got you there doesn’t matter. Most people in my experience wised up quickly to easy visual heuristics (DPR/IPR and others) they could get away with to solve RPM problems. For the mini-projects, there were a handful of people who worked ahead very quickly, figured out short cuts to solving them all, and then described the easiest solutions on Ed. Others will rightly point out that when it comes to learning you get out what you put in, but when you’re assigned 4-8 pages to write every single week, it’s hard to not take the easy path when it’s sitting right there in front of you. Speaking of which… -- There’s just too much writing. Some of the writing is fun (I had a blast writing about consciousness for homework 3), but the RPM journals get tedious. There are four milestone journals PLUS the big writeup for the final project. This is in addition to writeups for each of the five mini-projects, the three (long) homeworks, AND the peer feedback you have two write each week. I much rather would have spent more time coding.

    Overall, I’m right on the fence with recommending or not recommending this course. Would I take it again? In its current form, I don’t think so. It’s just way too easy to not learn very much, and I wish there were more mechanisms in place to hold students’ feet to the fire. However, if you’ve got time to put the work in and feel you won’t succumb to the temptation of taking the easy route on assignments, I can imagine this being a rewarding experience.

    Rating: 3 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 20 hours / week

  • TvFSsBSeLnM/oOffxQ2WtA==December 22, 2022fall 2022

    Very well taught class. It was my first in the OMSCS and I recommend it.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 13 hours / week

  • sZtrhGsP3CIA1RI1GIwOrw==December 15, 2022fall 2022

    KBAI was one of the best courses I've ever taken, and I highly recommend it for anyone who REALLY wants to learn how to think differently about complex problems (both in AI but also in a general technical atmosphere). Let me list the pros and cons below.

    Pros: • You learn a LOT. From the moment you enter the class to the day you leave, you are constantly learning about the parallels in how humans think and how we can mimic AI machines to think like us! Very fascinating stuff. • The course project is for sure a shiny badge for your resume. The semester-long Raven's Progressive Matrices Agent that you build will definitely teach you a lot about the course subjects, but it'll also look really good on your professional profiles or resume! Even if your Agent isn't in the top percentile, the learning you do and all the code you write for it over the semester is for sure an achievement regardless. • The Mini-Projects allow you to improve your coding. I knew programming decently well coming into this class, but after completing the coding projects needed, my python programming skills have improved drastically! • The sense of community on Ed is like nothing else. Seriously, the Ed forum was a lifesaver for this class. The professor and TA pretty much always foster a community of learning and making mistakes and helping eachother out. I can't recall a single time where they took down a post where students were helping eachother think about problem solving techniques and offering advice. You could tell that most of the teaching staff wanted students to succeed and help one another. • In my personal opinion, I think it's extremely important to have good written communication skills as a developer, and with all the reports you write in this course it definitely gives you the opportunity to work on and hone those skills. Writing reports for every single project can seem annoying at times, but I assure you'll come out a better writer! • You can tell the Professor loves this course and is dedicated to student success. Dr. Joyner is amazing at what he does, and it's clear throughout every point in this class. From the weekly assignment reminders, to the fun engaging lectures, and the occasional responses on Ed posts, I liked knowing I was in a class where I felt appreciated and valued as a student. • You WILL get out from this course what you put IN. If you make a decent effort to learn the concepts, complete the course project, and all the other assignments, you will come out of this class feeling like a different person. You will be equipped with a huge toolbelt of knowledge to go and tackle problems you never thought you could before. I came in knowing nothing about computer vision, and now I'm able to do technical analysis on images and process pixel counts and use those to make assumptions about what an image is showing me... very cool stuff! • You have the ability to work ahead! The thing I loved the most about this course was having the entire class given to you day one. You have a syllabus and deadlines for assignments, but you as a Student are able to pick how far ahead you want to work. This saved me so many times while working fulltime, because there were weeks where work or sickness caught hold of me but I had worked 2-3 weeks ahead and so I was seldom worried about my assignments. If you can, I HIGHLY implore you to get ahead wherever you can in this class. Some students worked so far ahead they got the entire month of December off and to themselves. So keep that in mind when taking this class.

    Cons: • Lecture Exercises are somewhat outdated (it can get kind of annoying because by the end of the semester I wasn't even actively doing them as I knew most of the results would be wrong anyways). Just focus on the lectures themselves. • Not necessarily a con, but rather a warning. You REALLY need to be a competent programmer. I don't recommend this class for anyone just learning how to code. A lot of the projects in this class require intensive programming. • Grading isn't very standardized. I only encountered this issue towards the very end of the course, but despite my reports being extremely similar in structure and format from week-to-week, some TA's may take that as a 100, and other TA's may take that as a 75. • Exams are fair, but my only hesitance with them is the way the questions are worded. I don't know how non-native english speakers even take them, as being one myself made it extremely difficult to understand the question wording a lot of the time. Hopefully they improve that in the future but for now, question wording can get really convoluted and you'll spend 2-3 minutes just deciphering what the question is asking. (Exam times are also on the shorter side so beware of that). • Peer Reviews did not help. In this class you're required to submit peer reviews for others' reports as part of participation. For one, most of the peer reviews I got didn't help me at all, but also in my cohort it actually caused issues in regards to retaliation. Students who took critical feedback would then go and "reputation strike" other students, which I think at the end caused a lot of issues that could be avoided outright if reviews are abolished or at least give reviewers option to remain anonymous.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • hzUZmfyoW8cRBEln8IJT/g==December 14, 2022fall 2022

    My first course in the program. It's an excellent "re-entry" into school (20 years past since undergraduate). I loved the concepts of thinking about problem solving and the direct links between human cognition and the algorithms implemented by computer "AI agents". It really expanded how I think about computer program design and the parallels to human thinking/problem solving. The programming itself wasn't too difficult. I improved my python skills immensely - but YMMV. My biggest complaint was the writing components - the weekly papers covering your program did become a bit of a chore. The class message forum was lively, helpful and educational.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 13 hours / week

  • ZN9uVRB/TlU1+5KZLa6fUA==December 13, 2022spring 2022

    I review each aspect of this class in detail here, in this 5 min video https://youtu.be/mCsmaXxZL60 Cheers!

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 12 hours / week

  • mIk9ODXqlIUCHcHB5PIMdw==December 12, 2022fall 2022

    Before I begin my review, I should let you know that I am not in the OMSCS program. I am in the C-track (Computational Data Analytics) for the OMSA program and took this course because C-track students have to take 2 CS courses for the program. This course got one of the better reviews of the ten CS course options listed for the C-track electives, so I decided to take this course. I'm disclosing this to let you know that I do not come from a pure CS background like most people that take this class, just to give you a heads-up.

    Now for the review. To put it simply, this class just has way too much going on. I knew going into this class that there would be a decent amount of writing because a lot of the reviews on here mentioned it. But what most of those reviews don't mention is EXACTLY how much writing there is, which is what I am going to tell you right now so that you know. Don't say I didn't warn you.

    Excluding the 2 exams, there are 13 assignments in total for this course, with all of them requiring a write-up. Here are the page total requirements for the assignments:

    • 5 papers for a semester-long coding project: 5 pages per paper for the first 4, and then up to 10 pages for the 5th one.
    • 5 papers for 5 different coding mini-projects: Up to 4 pages per paper
    • 3 papers for 3 different theory-based homework assignments based on lecture material: Up to 6 pages per paper (4 pages recommended)

    In total, I did roughly 65 pages of paper writing for this course. I should note that not every page was all text. You are asked on some papers to include figures/diagrams, which can take up at least half of a page a lot of the time. But there was definitely more text than figures in my papers.

    A paper is due every week, first thing Monday morning. And THEN you have to give peer feedback to 3 different students' papers that are randomly assigned to you for each of the 13 assignments. And the peer feedbacks that you submit are graded. If a student's paper meets all of the rubric's expectations and is really good, you can't simply write "This was a great submission and it met all of the expectations. Great job!" You will get a bad grade for that. You have to go into great detail and write in paragraphs why that student's submission was really good in order to get a good grade on your feedback, which I thought was redundant busy-work.

    All of this writing makes it incredibly easy to fall behind on the lecture videos, which are fairly interesting but they're only helpful for the exams in my opinion. I thought there was a pretty big disconnect between the lecture material and the coding assignments for this course. They were incredibly high-level "whiteboard" explanations that didn't really give any good algorithm breakdowns or coding whatsoever. I literally didn't use any of the concepts covered in the course for the semester-long project because the project was too big and complex for any of these concepts to be applicable. And this seemed to be the class consensus based on discussions in the class forum. I would encourage you to pay attention to the discussions in the class forum because students share methods that they implemented in their mini-projects and semester-long project that really helped them out, and I found that to be pretty useful.

    There's also no starter code for any of the coding assignments in this class - I didn't find this to be a big deal seeing as it is a grad-level CS course, but I figured I would just let you know this ahead of time as well.

    The one good thing about this class is that because there are so many assignments in the course, everything is weighted pretty evenly and lightly. So doing really poorly on one assignment is not gonna affect your grade that much. I got roughly 60% on the semester-long project for this course and I'm either going to barely get an A or easily get a B in the course.

    In terms of coding, I would say it is on the easier side for OMSCS students (it's all in Python, by the way). But my final advice to you regarding this course is this: if you are not required to take this course for your program, you need to SERIOUSLY consider whether you are willing to do a ton of excessive writing - between assignment write-ups, peer feedback, and coding - for a 4-month period. Because that's what you're gonna spend the vast majority of time doing in this course.

    Rating: 1 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 20 hours / week

  • 7IdZ6iwCLVyY305YF5nhDA==November 14, 2022spring 2022

    Knowledge-Based AI was my second/third course only after AI. I would like to say what I really loved about KBAI. The course material was easy to understand and digest. The projects were pretty great and while we had to think about them, they weren't that difficult to solve or submit. The TAs were really generous and helped out a lot. For an online student, the best tool that we used was Ed Discussions and Peer Feedback, as these were a semblance of actually interacting with the class. The students were extremely helpful and may be due to participation points, were really supportive throughout.

    There was not much I didn't like, but I would definitely say that this was a time-consuming class. Weekly Projects, Reports, and Submissions without any breaks were extremely exhausting. Even if I missed one week, I would lose a grade. Not saying that a Masters's Course should be any less rigorous but this is something the student should expect from this class.

    The only reason for bumping difficulty from 3 to 4 is to keep it aligned with the strict time-bound activities we need to perform. Overall I had the most fun in this class and would recommend students to take this class.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • R2heKw+FhNUo9VKLa/P0Qg==November 5, 2022fall 2022

    I'm taking this as a first OMSCS course, with 2 STEM degrees, some undergraduate CS coursework, and a functional (but rusty) grasp of python.

    Noting that folks of widely ranging backgrounds take this course, I'd like to address some common criticisms.

    1. There is too much writing. There is an assignment due basically every Sunday midnight (except exam weeks). Nearly every assignment is 50% Gradescope performance and 50% write up. If you bomb an assignment, it's not the end of the world.

    The write-ups are really not bad, and very manageable for graduate level coursework. Maybe 3-4 hrs spread over the week.

    • Use a latex template (Overleaf is very easy) from the beginning.
    • Stick to the posted rubric.
    • Make plots / tables as you do the projects. You'll find yourself hitting the page limit in basically every write-up.
    • Don't wait till Sunday to write it or you'll hate every moment.
    • The peer reviews on your write up are actually quite motivating.

    1. They don't teach you how to program. Yeah, not really. This isn't a programming class, it's a graduate-level classical AI course. They cover a range of topics on a high level, then toss you interesting projects to get your hands dirty. The discussion forums offer plenty of help if you're struggling. (there is usually an easy way to complete a project, but it's fun to try some exotic stuff out).

    2. This is all outdated anyway. I never understand this take. A survey of classical AI techniques is going to broaden your understanding of the field and give you a wider base of approaches to apply to future problems.

    There isn't a clear 1:1 relation from classical AI to modern data-driven approaches. But if you really can't fathom how to use anything but deep learning to solve problems, you're part of the problem.

    To wrap up: If you're here to learn and want a good class to sink your teeth into (that isn't going to absolutely kill you); you're in the right place.

    If you're here to get in and get out, well, its not crazy hard and you can get by with just skimming the lectures. You might actually enjoy it, so be careful of that.

    The class felt a little front loaded. It's progression and organization are great, but they jump right into it with the first project. If you're not comfortable programming you'll have to learn quickly.

    You'll have to be pragmatic with your time to get projects finished. Get a basic, working version submitted early before scaling it up.

    I absolutely recommend this class. But don't take it if:

    • You don't have at least a functional knowledge of python or object oriented programming. Learning this right before the class is going to be a hard sell unless you're comfortable in similar languages.

    • You want a theory class. This is basically all projects and not much math.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • BWYOWLQN/EfIJBSyM83LGA==October 9, 2022fall 2022

    Just took the first exam of the semester. I'm taking this class with a background in computer science, while also balancing a full-time job and a supportive family. It's my first class in the program too.

    Let's start with the negatives of the class. First, speaking as a writer, there is a LOT of writing. Heed this warning. You will be programming a lot, AND writing a lot, AND reviewing people's writing a lot. This is a class that demands 50% of your energy for writing + reading and 50% for programming. If you're okay with this, then that's awesome. If not, then you should consider a class that doesn't require you to write so extensively. I think I've written about 30 pages worth of stuff by now, and it's been only a month.

    The second thing, which is more serious--there have been a LOT of criticisms regarding how, despite a large number of our assignments requiring us to program extensively, there are very little to no programmatic algorithms introduced to us in the class. Which is a little awkward, considering many people recommend KBAI as a first class for people--in this sense, it's probably better as a second-semester class.

    There's sooo many people who say "This is grad school. Don't expect to be spoonfed anything." but this criticism isn't coming from people who want to be spoonfed. It's coming from people who want, literally, perhaps a page from Russel & Norvig or a link to some reference for generic pseudocode and programmatic explanations. There are absolutely NO guidelines at all on how to program some of the algorithms presented in the course! I would be incredibly surprised if you could prove me wrong on this!

    I personally have had to do the added research of perusing Russell and Norvig, as well as the optional readings from our Winston textbook and studying relevant data structures that may outline how to approach the assignment, in order to get an A.

    If you take this, PLEASE be aware that you will not receive any help from the lectures themselves on how to approach programming assignments, much less learning Python! I use Python at my job on the regular, and I cannot imagine taking this course if you've never used Python!

    As for the positives, there are many. Perhaps as a result of writing all of these reports, if you take this as a first class into the program, you will have set up a good process + environment for writing papers in the future. All of the assignments are given to you at the start of the semester, so you can plan ahead as needed. TAs are incredibly responsive; exams are fair. Programming exercises, if you know how to program beforehand at least, illuminate the concepts in the lecture very well. They are also very flexible exercises--you aren't being shoehorned into a very specific technique or method in order to get 100%.

    I just don't know if these positives can outweigh this HUGE, GLARING negative point. I think it wouldn't be so serious if the course instructors themselves emphasized that it would be very, very important to have prior understanding of Python and a basic grasp of data structures, or suggest as optional reading some more technical papers that outline a programmatic approach to some of the algorithms from lecture, or at the very least a few chapters from the AI textbook by Russell and Norvig.

    I struggled to get a grasp for the class at the beginning of the semester because of how lost I felt for our first programming assignment, but I was able to figure things out by reading a lot more and doing my own research, so I'm much more comfortable now. But there are still a lot of students I've seen in the discussion forums and in our peer reviews who are struggling with completing the programming exercises because they are very genuinely lost, and it's hard to blame them.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • 7tWp0/tvApjYBN8jUeMYBw==October 9, 2022fall 2022

    this was my first class in the program and what a fantastic class to kick things off with. Very interesting and extremely well structured and enjoyable. Dr. Joyner remains my favorite humans in the program and I highly recommend the class to anyone looking to get an understanding of AI and practical coding. Thank you Dr. Joyner for making my life easier and doing a good job structuring your classes so well

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • Y1SPMsEBOM8xa+gjCWAxMw==September 18, 2022fall 2022

    Nightmare.

    1. Like said in the other reviews, this course teaches a very out-of-date concept. I don't think anyone is really using this techniques in the industry.
    2. Maybe because of the lack of real knowledge in this course, it has lots of tedious homework. For example, in Homework 1 Question 2, it asks to read some European Regulations, and how it applies to general AI, etc. which is completely irrelevant to the course. (More like an ethical course topic)
    3. Again, maybe because of the boredom of this course, it asks to complete weekly reports and so-called peer feedback.

    Overall, this is a very tedious course teaching useless content. It has a public website (Google it). I highly recommend you read the website before taking this course.

    Rating: 1 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 20 hours / week

  • qRuarEhU/+mlCa5553Sjmg==August 19, 2022spring 2022

    Positives-

    1. Well run, organised
    2. Was my first course, so good warm up to the rest of the degree
    3. Everything is thrown open on day one, so you can plan the course as per your pace
    4. Good projects to pick up python programming fluency
    5. You can score well, possibly an A, if you just follow the instructions. Major chunk of the marks is reports for the projects which are easily to do if you just follow the grid.

    Negatives-

    1. Course is not relevant to modern day AI, or something which will help you at work (or secure a new job)
    2. Re-stressing again on the above negative, very theoretical and intuitive topics discussed in the course, not directly applicable in your programming
    3. Although the projects are good at building fluency and logic, they are general programming challenges and independent of the course lectures to a certain extent. You can use common sense, any algorithm, or force the course topics into the projects but this is reinforces my above contention that the topics are not relevant to modern programming
    4. Every week or so a paper is due, can find it a bit too much
    5. Exams tested less of conceptual learning and were more focussed on command over English language to decipher the 'tricky worded' questions

    Rating: 2 / 5Difficulty: 2 / 5Workload: 13 hours / week

  • zUrN/pXcrShtibFvUgnPfg==August 19, 2022summer 2022

    This course was great for learning about topics used in KBAI, but you do not have to code most of the topics and can get away with doing simple solutions for the projects. If you are someone who can only learn topics by coding them, this class might not help you to learn. The test cases for the projects are fairly simple so your code can be inefficient and use basic search instead of the topics taught in the class. The workload is a lot compared to other classes. There is often a coding assignment, report about the coding assignment, homework essays, and lectures to complete during the week. There are also exams, which are thankfully open notes, and a semester-long project that will require a lot of time to complete. I would say this class is not difficult but tedious and an unnecessary amount of work to learn the topics. Overall, I would recommend taking this class if it satisfies a requirement but finding an easier class if you do not have a lot of time during the week for assignments.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • Georgia Tech StudentMay 8, 2022spring 2022

    This course has extensive load. I have to spent nearly 20 hours the first half just to keep up with the schedule. The mini-projects can be tough and has nothing to do with what is taught in lecture. The workload is heavy. The 3 homework is also not very relevant and confusing rubric. The good side is the grading is reasonable. As long as you write something that make sense, you are expected to get full score. The Final RPM seems a duplicate of previous 4 RPM, but has a substantial weight of 15%, equal to two exams. It is very helpful to read through previous reviewers to have some expectation. I had thought this is a easy course and didn't start work on projects until late of wk2, and have felt some pressure to keep up.I personally think the 3 homework is simply waste of time or weighted too much. 5% each, and I usually have to spend a whole week to finish writing. Also, reviewing 6 papers from your peers every week is also not making much sense. I felt less motivated to prepare for exam 2. Did some calculation and knowing that I will still getting A even if I score 20 for the exam.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • Georgia Tech StudentMay 7, 2022spring 2022

    Pros-

    1. Interactive course, keeps you engaged
    2. Very well structured and organised, Dr Joyner is an expert in online education!
    3. Great community support, you will not have to struggle alone
    4. Programming projects are interesting (if you like Python programming)
    5. Thought process and logic of KBAI would help you refine your programming logic and style
    6. You can pace the course as per your convenience, everything is thrown open in the very beginning.

    Cons-

    1. Not a cakewalk or easy course to get an A
    2. Atleast one report is due every week
    3. You have to spend substantial hours for report writing, peer reviews and programming
    4. Programming projects are not beginner level and will be steep if you are new to programming or not fluent in algorithms.
    5. KBAI school of AI is a bit outdated and doesnt see high usage/deployment in modern day programming.

    Summary- This is not an easy A course as provided by some reviewers, first 1-2 months of the course can be overwhelming and you must be ready to dedicate time and efforts to overcome that. After this the course becomes relatively easier to handle.

    However course is well organised and well designed and if you are willing to give your time, be active in the forums, you will be able to well. Unlike some courses, efforts will ensure you a high performance in the course and Dr Joyner expertise in online education is amply reflected in the course.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 17 hours / week

  • Georgia Tech StudentMay 5, 2022spring 2022

    Background: Chemical Engineering w/minimal python experience

    Workload: as a non-CS background, this was a good first coding class in the OMSCS program. The mini-projects are challenging, but my coding has improved immensely. Like I look at my first project and can not believe how far I've come understanding and utilizing classes for object oriented programming. The caveat is that I spent a lot of time learning fundamentals which upped my hours/week. If you have background in this, or just want to get the projects done, you can definitely spend less time than me per week. For reference, the first mini-project took me 20+ hours while others in the forums mentioned it took them 5 hours. Also note that the class seems pretty front loaded, or at least it was for me. The tail end of the course I spent WAY less hours per week, but that could also be due to the fact that I took time in the beginning to learn fundamentals.

    Grading: The forums are fairly open for discussion and support, minus actual code sharing, which is great for learning and expanding your approaches. The head TA, Kia, was amazing. Quick responses and very helpful. They just started using a rubric for grading, so there is no ambiguity in what you will be graded on. Note that each assignment has a journal aspect. This is great if you are weak at coding as each assignment grade is only 50% code. It is not great if English is your second language/you hate writing. Overall, I found grading fair. If you put in decent effort, you can make it out of this class with an A or B.

    Material: seriously, these are the most interesting lectures I have ever watched. By far the most interesting class I have ever taken. I can not recommend this course enough. I wasn't entirely sure what the class was about when I signed up, but imagine breaking human cognition down to the most finite, subconscious elements. For example, you need to paint a ceiling and the ladder. We instantly know you must paint the ceiling first, otherwise the ladder will be wet and you can't climb it. But how do our brains make that decision? How do we break that down to a method to code into an intelligent agent? Another example: you need a cup from the kitchen, but all the cups are dirty. How do you improvise and know that bowl might be used instead? In fact, how do you even know that a yellow cup and a cup with a handle are both examples of cups? How do we learn and how do we correct mistakes in our learning? A lot of ML/AI requires thousands of data points to train on, but as humans, we often don't have that as we learn, so how do we proceed through the world with limited knowledge. Seriously, this stuff has me agreeing with Musk on simulation theory or with Sam Harris on the fact that free will is an illusion.

    Prep: If you aren't familiar with object-oriented programming, I highly recommend watching the section here on breadth-first search (https://cs50.harvard.edu/ai/2020/weeks/0/). The class never mentions it, and I was lucky to see it in the forum. My biggest issue with the class is not introducing this as a more concrete coding application rather than the theoretical stuff they propose.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 20 hours / week

  • Georgia Tech StudentMay 4, 2022spring 2022

    This class' difficulty is based on how you approach the course. If you'd like to actually apply what is taught in the lectures, then the projects are a bit involved. But 4 out of the 5 mini-projects can easily be "cheesed" with really simple solutions, because the test cases on GradeScope are so simple. RPM is a bit involved in the beginning getting set up. As the project progresses and you have to code more, you can observe other classmate's strategies through Peer Feedback and get ideas for implementations. A lot of people complain about having to learn Pillow/OpenCV, but if you are used to reading documentation and experimenting to learn an API, it's really not that bad.

    The way to make this course as easy as possible is to get high A's on all the assignments so that a low final project performance score is fine. My RPM performance scored a 55/100, but I was still easily able to get an A.

    TA staff is very engaged and helpful, and students are very collaborative on the forum, which is really nice. Nothing is redacted or censored. The first half of the course content is well-defined and concrete. The second half becomes a bit more nebulous, but is somewhat interesting.

    The worst part of this course is really the exams. They are incredibly vague and wordy for no reason. I have no idea how a non-native speaker would be able to do well on these. Even with them being open-book, open-internet, deciphering what is being said is quite difficult. You also never get to see what was scored as correct/incorrect after grades are released, so it is a bit of a mystery what a correct answer usually looks like. The second exam was a bit more vague than the first exam.

    Also, Peer Feedback requirements are excessive. This course would really benefit if they halved the required feedback amount.

    Overall this is the ideal first OMSCS course if you are new to CS. The programming is straightforward, the class is very well-run, TAs are awesome, and all assignments are released at the beginning of the semester with no revisions to instructions.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 2 / 5Workload: 8 hours / week

  • Georgia Tech StudentMay 2, 2022spring 2022

    Really interesting course that teachings you valuable building blocks. Good amount of paper writing. Lectures videos are the best of any course I've taken. Projects are good, just make sure you watch the relevant lessons first. Work on the PRM project early if possible.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 2 / 5Workload: 10 hours / week

  • Georgia Tech StudentApril 22, 2022spring 2022

    Amazing class, highly recommend. It was super exciting learning about how humans may approach and solve problems, and then translating it over to computers. They cover a wide range of topics which combine cognitive science and computer science. The videos are broken down into manageable chunks and have many helpful exercises with follow-up explanations. TAs and professor are very active on the forum and responsive. Assignments and final project draw from course lectures. There are opportunities to earn extra participation, capped at 100% for the category. No curve, no extra credit. Full course calendar is available from the beginning of class, but there isn't much room to work ahead.

    The first half of the class balances between coding and written assignments, and has more cohesive content. They go over knowledge representation structures and different problem solving techniques, and keep referencing them in later lectures and incorporating them into all of the written assignments so you really get a solid foundation.

    The second half of the class branches out into many different topics, and with all of the coding assignments bunched together in the middle of the term, feels a little less cohesive. You do something different every week and start to forget what you learned from previous weeks. They do reference previous material, but not as much, so it doesn't stick as well as material from the first half of the course. That being said, I still learned a lot and highly recommend this class.

    Background:

    • undergrad CS background
    • work full time
    • number of previous classes: 0
    • other classes this semester: 0

    Breakdown of average time commitment per week:

    • 2 lessons. 1 hour to watch videos (add 1 or 2 hours for understanding the material)
    • 1 assignment, either coding + report or written homework. 20 hours
    • 3 required peer reviews. 1 hour
    • participation, easiest way to earn with confidence on number of points is with 3 extra peer reviews. 1 hour

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 24 hours / week

  • Georgia Tech StudentApril 9, 2022spring 2022

    This is my first OMS course. I can safely say that this is a great course to take as an introduction to the program as a whole and is a great way to get a solid grade for your first foundational course requirement. Though, if you do not have CS experience (university or a minor) or are working full-time then this course may be more difficult. The lectures are digestible and are broken up by mini quizzes that test your understanding of the material throughout. Compared to my previous institution’s online lectures, these were like a Hollywood produced movies. Dr. Ashok and Dr. Joyner are great lecturers too.

    There are only 2 exams in this course, both of which are open note and open book. My only critique about these is that the questions are very obtuse and require sometimes very creative applications of what is learned in the lecture.

    Aside from that, most of the assignments you’ll be graded on will be Homework, mini-projects, participation, and the Raven’s Progressive Matrices.

    Homework assignments are essays that will sometimes ask you to answer a philosophical question about cognition and how said concept can be described to a computer. These assignments took the most time to complete since it was recommended to write up to 4-6 pages on these topics but they were also one of the easiest.

    Mini-projects are the second highlight of this course. There are 5 of them all of which are based on the lectures. I would recommend not tackling these in the way the lecture explicitly tells you to tackle them. It’s better to try to solve them as you would naturally, and you’ll always end up solving it in a way the lecture expects you. In other words, don’t think too hard about how to approach them.

    Participation is based on how active you are on Ed (discussion board for asking questions and what not), giving peer review feedback on assignments, submitting example questions for exams the list goes on. If you want to max out your participation early on, I recommend doing a lot of peer review feedback. I wouldn’t recommend stopping though because some peers may have some insight that can help you for Raven’s Progressive Matrices.

    Raven’s Progressive Matrices is the main highlight of this course. You’re building a program that can pass a visual IQ exam. This project is broken up into 5 parts and each part tests a specific section of the IQ exam. Ultimately, if you want to do well on these assignments I recommend looking into Pillow, OpenCV, and image analysis functions like dark pixel ratios.

    One more thing I forgot to add. Mini-projects and Raven's Progressive Matrices Milestones requires a report of what was accomplished and what you learned that's usually 4-6 pages long. So if you are not a fan of writing 800-1000 words a week then this course may not be for you. On that note, some of the lecture topics aren't applicable for the mini-projects or Raven's Progressive Matrices even though they are advertised as so. The concepts only work when applied in an abstract way, but sometimes when programming your A.I. you may accidentally programming it the way the lectures says it should be done if you don't think about it too hard.

    Grades take a long time to be published with many taking 3.5 weeks or more.

    In conclusion, I highly recommend this course to those first starting off in the OMS program. It gets you acclimated to discussion boards and graduate level expectations without the fear of not fulfilling the foundation course requirement of a B or higher.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 2 / 5Workload: 19 hours / week

  • Georgia Tech StudentFebruary 1, 2022fall 2021

    I took this class as my first class in the program because the reviews here said that it was a good intro class to the program and with fairly low workload. That was not my experience at all. I am a CS major and work as a software engineer and even though the coding assignments were not a problem per se (other than learning some new libraries), the amount of writing in this class is insane. There are two homework where I spent at least 12 hours researching each. It was a subject not even related to the lectures, it was more related to ethical AI which I didn't think this class was about. I don't think the lectures actually help you solve the final project, so you ave to put it a lot of extra time to come up with a solution. I passed with an A, but I put in a lot of time. If you don't like writing a lot of papers and doing mandatory peer reviews, stay away of the class. I will say I liked the class though. I do think it was interesting but it was not what I was expecting based on the reviews here. The good side of the class is that it extremely well organized and you can start doing the assignments as soon as you want, which can put some people in advantage to work on the final project if they have the time.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 30 hours / week

  • Georgia Tech StudentJanuary 20, 2022spring 2021

    This class was harder than I expected due to the fact that the semester I took it was the first semester they added mini projects. The mini projects weren't all that bad overall, but some of them made me have to refresh myself on AI algorithms and the likes. For one of them though, I was able to come up with a quick 11 line solution so I'd say definitely don't overthink any of these, brute forcing is the way to go. The RPM semester-long project was definitely harder but the best things you can do are implementing basic transformations, boolean operations (OR, AND, etc), and Dark Pixel Ratio (DPR) and just taking it one milestone at a time. I was able to get around 70% accuracy with these alone. It was pretty satisfying knowing I had created an agent that could solve the majority of a set of human intelligence questions. The lectures are pretty decent, though I wish they would redo them in the style of HCI with my man Joyner taking the lead. Overall, this is definitely the better AI course in the program even if it may not be as traditional as CS 6601.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 20 hours / week

  • Georgia Tech StudentJanuary 17, 2022fall 2021

    This was my first OMSCS class, and I had different expectations going into the class than what I actually received from it. I almost dropped the class upon failing to implement knowledge from the lecture into mini project one, which I explain in the next paragraph. Tough it out, as I got a 97% (A) in the end. The first few weeks are overwhelming. Then somewhere in the middle, there are about 5 weeks where it gets SUPER chill. Then the last 2 weeks, it is slightly hectic, but nothing like the first few weeks.

    Tips: The first mini project was incredibly difficult because I tried to solve it by incorporating stuff from the lecture. But if you ignore the lectures and solve the mini projects using methods that are not necessarily perfect, but rather "good enough", you will realize that this entire course is a lot easier than it seems at first. Remember that the goal of the class is to mimic human intelligence. So sometimes, your code is allowed to reflect some of those human imperfections, too.

    The same applies to RPM. But anyways, RPM was by far the most annoying part of the course, just because I had to learn computer vision with Pillow. Most checks you will implement are high-level, so Pillow is good enough to get a 100 on the project.

    Also obsessing about time taken for your agents to run is not important for any of the mini projects, or RPM. For example, I know most people's RPM agents ran in under 10 seconds. Mine took 90-100 seconds but I didn't get any points docked off for my agent being slower. Only time I got points docked off was for not mentioning the time at all on a mini project, and it was only docked from the report.

    Last but not least, expect to write ~10 pages every week, give or take a few pages here and there depending on the assignment. You will have to not only write functioning code, but then describe it in a report/document. The homeworks are the most frustrating to write, even though you do not have to code anything. You will have to draw diagrams, independently research relevant topics, and then tie it all in with a lecture.

    Overall, I liked the class but was extremely frustrated with the amount of writing and doing RPM as a final project because it has a learning curve that the class workload does not account for very well. Great first class, especially for those in the Interactive Intelligence specialization, as it will be one of two required courses for them.
